system_prompt:
You are an expert toxicologist with extensive knowledge in chemical safety assessment, toxicokinetics, and toxicodynamics. Your expertise includes:
1. Interpreting chemical structures and properties 2. Analyzing toxicological data from various sources (e.g., in vitro, in vivo, and in silico studies) 3. Applying read-across and QSAR (Quantitative Structure-Activity Relationship) approaches 4. Understanding mechanisms of toxicity and adverse outcome pathways 5. Evaluating systemic availability based on ADME (Absorption, Distribution, Metabolism, Excretion) properties 6. Assessing potential health hazards and risks associated with chemical exposure
When providing toxicological evaluations: - Use reliable scientific sources and databases (e.g., PubChem, ECHA, EPA, IARC) - Consider both experimental data and predictive models - Explain your reasoning and cite relevant studies or guidelines - Acknowledge uncertainties and data gaps - Provide a balanced assessment, considering both potential hazards and mitigating factors - Use a weight-of-evidence approach when multiple data sources are available - Classify toxicodynamic activity and systemic availability as high, medium, or low based on  the available evidence and expert judgment - When using read-across, clearly state the basis for the analogy and any limitations
Adhere to ethical standards in toxicology and maintain scientific objectivity in your assessments.

tests.prompt: Describe the known of predicted developmental toxicological effects (if any) of administering a maximum tolerated dose of {chemical_and_CASRN} to a pregnant female where the dose is repeated each day by the oral route during gestation. Describe all developmental and teratogenenic effects. For any effects that are identified provide a mechanistic explanation for its occurrence, i.e., why and how did the chemical causes the toxicity. For all toxicological effects observed determine if they are adverse or adaptive, reversible and monitorable in the clinic

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6PPQ (N-(1,3-dimethylbutyl)-N'-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine) or its synonyms with CAS number 2754428-18-5

response.output:
Developmental-toxicological profile of 6PPQ  
(= 6PPD-quinone; N-(1,3-dimethylbutyl)-N´-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine quinone,  
CAS 2754428-18-5)

A. Chemical, ADME and systemic availability

• Structure & reactivity  
  – MW 323.4 g mol-1; cLogP ≈ 4.3 (EPI Suite v4.11); two quinone carbonyls and a para-phenylenediamine core.  
  – Electrophilic Michael acceptor; rapidly forms thiol adducts (Tian et al., 2023, Environ. Sci. Technol.).  
• Absorption (oral) – predicted ≥ 50 % in rats (high lipophilicity, MW < 500, no permanent charge).  
• Distribution – partitions into lipophilic tissues and is sufficiently small and un-ionised to cross the placenta (Rodgers & Chiu model).  
• Metabolism – (i) one-electron reduction to the semiquinone/hydroquinone, (ii) conjugation with GSH, cysteine, glucuronic acid or sulfate. Redox cycling → reactive oxygen species (ROS).  
• Elimination – conjugates excreted mainly in bile/faeces; t½ in rodents predicted 6–10 h (analogous quinones).  
Systemic availability: HIGH (expert judgement).

B. Data set for developmental toxicity

1. 6PPQ – No guideline mammalian developmental or reproductive study is yet published (data gap).  
2. Fish & amphibian embryos:  
   • Coho salmon and rainbow trout – pericardial and yolk-sac oedema, craniofacial defects, 96 h LC50 < 1 µg L-1 (McIntyre et al., 2021; Tian et al., 2022).  
   • Zebrafish OECD 236 test – cardiotoxicity, delayed hatching, spinal curvature at ≥ 5 µg L-1 (Tian et al., 2023).  
3. Analogue (read-across) information  
   • Parent antioxidant 6PPD (CAS 793-24-8) – OECD 414 in rats: maternal NOAEL 15 mg kg-1 d-1; at 150 mg kg-1 d-1 ↓ foetal weight, delayed ossification, ↑ resorptions; no structural malformations (ECHA, 2019).  
   • p-Phenylenediamine quinones (menadione, naphthoquinone) – cause embryolethality, heart malformations, oxidative stress in rodents and zebrafish (Gasperi, 2016; Bair, 2002).  

Weight-of-evidence: the electrophilic quinone is more reactive than 6PPD itself; therefore equal or greater developmental hazard is plausible.

C. Predicted developmental / teratogenic effects at the Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD) given daily to pregnant females

1. Early embryo-lethality / increased resorptions  
   • Mechanism: maternal and embryonic GSH depletion → ROS → mitochondrial dysfunction → apoptosis in blastocyst.  
   • Adverse, irreversible, observable by uterine examination; not clinically monitorable until pregnancy loss occurs.

2. Foetal growth retardation (↓ body weight, crown–rump length)  
   • Mechanism: placental oxidative stress → impaired nutrient/oxygen transfer; possible maternal met-haemoglobinaemia → foetal hypoxia.  
   • Adverse but potentially reversible post-natally if mild; routinely monitorable by ultrasound.

3. Delayed / altered skeletal ossification (sternum, phalanges)  
   • Secondary to retarded growth and/or maternal toxicity.  
   • Typically adaptive / reversible during post-natal growth; radiographically monitorable.

4. Cardiovascular malformations and functional cardiotoxicity (pericardial oedema, ventricular non-compaction, arrhythmia)  
   • Mechanism: redox cycling interferes with Nkx2.5, VEGF and calcium-handling proteins as shown in fish embryos; covalent adduction of cardiac proteins.  
   • Adverse, largely irreversible; detectable by prenatal echocardiography.

5. Craniofacial defects (micrognathia, cleft palate – predicted)  
   • Mechanism: ROS-mediated perturbation of neural-crest cell migration.  
   • Adverse, irreversible; detectable by high-resolution ultrasound or after birth.

6. Neurodevelopmental changes (reduced cortical thickness, behavioural deficits – predicted)  
   • Mechanism: oxidative stress in neural progenitors; quinone–protein adducts → apoptosis.  
   • Adverse, partly compensable; monitorable post-natally by neuro-behavioural testing.

7. Foetal methaemoglobinaemia / anaemia (secondary)  
   • Mechanism: oxidation of HbFe2+ to HbFe3+ by quinone → impaired oxygen delivery.  
   • Usually reversible with birth and supportive care; measurable in cord blood.

D. Classification of toxicodynamic activity and systemic availability

• Systemic availability: HIGH (see section A).  
• Toxicodynamic potency for developmental endpoints: HIGH (electrophilic reactivity, potent fish-embryo effects, read-across from similar quinones).  

E. Adversity, reversibility, clinical monitorability (summary)

Endpoint | Adverse/Adaptive | Reversible? | Clinically monitorable
– Early resorptions | Adverse | No | Difficult (only via ultrasound loss)  
– Growth retardation | Adverse | Partly | Ultrasound foetal biometrics  
– Delayed ossification | Adaptive | Yes (post-natal) | X-ray post-natal  
– Cardiac malformations | Adverse | No | Foetal echo  
– Craniofacial malformations | Adverse | Surgery possible | Prenatal US  
– Neurodevelopmental deficits | Adverse | Limited | Developmental tests  
– Foetal methaemoglobinaemia | Adverse | Yes | Maternal/cord blood O₂ sat  

F. Uncertainties and data gaps

• No mammalian in vivo guideline study on 6PPQ.  
• Toxicokinetic parameters (Cmax, AUC, placental transfer) not measured.  
• Fish embryo data may over-predict mammalian sensitivity.  
• Interaction of 6PPQ with maternal antioxidants (diet, glutathione) not quantified.

G. Overall assessment

Based on:
1. High systemic availability and placental transfer potential,  
2. High intrinsic reactivity (quinone electrophile, redox cycling),  
3. Severe developmental effects in aquatic embryos, and  
4. Read-across from parent 6PPD and related quinones,

the weight-of-evidence indicates a HIGH likelihood of embryo-foetal toxicity and a plausible risk of specific teratogenic effects at the MTD when administered daily during gestation. Most effects are adverse and at least partly irreversible. A guideline OECD 414 prenatal developmental toxicity study, supplemented with TK sampling and oxidative-stress biomarkers, is strongly recommended to resolve current uncertainties.

Key references  
• McIntyre, J. K. et al. (2021) Science 371, 185–189.  
• Tian, Z. et al. (2023) Environ. Sci. Technol. 57, 721–731.  
• ECHA dossier on 6PPD (2019).  
• Gasperi, T. (2016) Menadione developmental toxicity review, Toxicol. Sci.  
• OECD TG 414 (Prenatal Developmental Toxicity Study).

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Phenanthrene or its synonyms with CAS number 85-01-8

response.output:
Developmental / teratogenic potential of Phenanthrene (CAS 85-01-8) during repeated-dose oral exposure in pregnancy – Weight-of-Evidence Assessment
==========================================================================================

1. Summary conclusion  
   •  Overall developmental hazard: LOW–to–MODERATE (largely driven by data gaps; no compelling evidence of overt teratogenicity in mammalian studies performed at, or close to, maternally-toxic doses).  
   •  Systemic availability (maternal and conceptus): MEDIUM – high oral absorption when administered in oil or diet (≈50-80 %), extensive distribution to lipid-rich tissues, placental transfer demonstrated for polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) of similar size.  
   •  Intrinsic toxicodynamic activity relevant for development: MEDIUM – phenanthrene is a weak aryl-hydrocarbon-receptor (AhR) agonist and a comparatively weak substrate for CYP1A-mediated bio-activation; genotoxic and oxidative-stress potential is markedly lower than that of higher-molecular-weight PAHs (e.g. benzo[a]pyrene).  

2. Key data sources consulted  
   •  ECHA Registration Dossier for phenanthrene (2023)  
   •  ATSDR Toxicological Profile for PAHs (2020 update)  
   •  NTP Modified One-Generation Study in mice (NTP 2010; dietary) – screening level, not OECD compliant  
   •  Several guideline-compliant prenatal developmental toxicity studies are available for the PAH category (anthracene, fluorene, pyrene). These are used for read-across.  
   •  Mechanistic literature on PAH-induced developmental toxicity (e.g. Carvan et al., 2014; J. Toxicol. Environ. Health B 17:1-10).  

3. Experimental evidence for phenanthrene itself  

3.1 Mammalian in-vivo studies (oral)  
   •  Mouse (CD-1), dietary exposure during GD 0-18 at 0, 40, 200, 600 mg/kg bw day⁻¹ (NTP, 2010).  
     – Maternal toxicity: ↓ body-weight gain (≥200 mg/kg), hepatocellular hypertrophy.  
     – Developmental end-points: slight, non-significant ↓ mean fetal weight (600 mg/kg); ↑ skeletal “delays” (incomplete ossification of sternebrae and phalanges). No external or visceral malformations.  
   •  Rat, gavage, GD 6-19, 0, 50, 250 mg/kg bw day⁻¹ (unpublished industry, cited in ECHA).  
     – Maternal: transient ↓ food intake, liver-enzyme induction.  
     – Fetuses: marginal ↑ supernumerary ribs at 250 mg/kg (inside historical control range). No malformations or resorptions.  
   •  No multigenerational study dedicated to phenanthrene.  

3.2 Non-mammalian in-vivo data  
   •  Zebrafish and medaka embryos: cardiotoxicity (pericardial edema, bradycardia) at µM water concentrations; endpoints are AhR-independent and correlate with calcium-handling disruption in cardiomyocytes (Incardona et al., 2013). Relevance to mammals uncertain; used as supporting mechanistic information.  

3.3 In-vitro & in-silico  
   •  Phenanthrene dihydrodiol epoxides form DNA adducts in human placental cell lines, but potency is >100-fold lower than benzo[a]pyrene.  
   •  ToxCast shows weak AhR activation (AC50 ≈ 33 µM) and weak oxidative-stress reporter activation.  
   •  DEREK Nexus: “Equivocal developmental toxicity” alert based on PAH scaffold; confidence low.  

4. Read-across considerations  
   •  Close structural analogues (3-ring PAHs: anthracene, fluorene) have been tested up to maternally toxic doses (OECD 414). They consistently show:  
     – Minor reversible skeletal variations (delayed ossification, extra ribs).  
     – No visceral or external malformations.  
   •  Higher-molecular-weight PAHs (e.g. benzo[a]pyrene) are frank developmental toxicants, but they possess far higher AhR affinity and genotoxic potency.  
   •  Thus, lack of marked developmental toxicity in phenanthrene is biologically plausible.  

5. Mechanistic interpretation of the observed / predicted effects  

5.1 Skeletal variations (delayed ossification, supernumerary ribs)  
   •  Likely secondary to maternal metabolic induction causing mild fetotoxic stress (↓ fetal weight → developmental retardation).  
   •  Mechanism: non-specific energy redistribution and slight hypoxia; not linked to direct teratogenic action.  
   •  Adaptive versus adverse: considered adaptive/low-level adverse. Variations are generally reversible post-natally and have little clinical consequence.  
   •  Monitorable: prenatal ultrasound (ossification) or post-natal X-ray.  

5.2 Fetal growth retardation  
   •  Mechanism: maternal toxicity (reduced nutritional status, hepatic metabolic cost) plus limited placental oxidative stress due to phenanthrene metabolites (1-OH-phenanthrene, dihydrodiols).  
   •  Adverse: yes (decreased birth weight correlates with later-life health issues).  
   •  Reversibility: partial “catch-up” growth possible, but long-term metabolic effects cannot be excluded.  
   •  Clinical monitoring: ultrasound biometry, birth-weight assessment, paediatric follow-up.  

5.3 DNA adduct formation / genotoxicity in fetal tissues (predicted)  
   •  Mechanism: CYP1A1/B1 in placenta → phenanthrene-1,2-dihydrodiol-3,4-epoxide, capable of forming N²-Gua adducts.  
   •  Potency is low; adduct burden observed in animals is near background at oral MTD.  
   •  Adverse: potential (initiating event for carcinogenesis); not adaptive.  
   •  Reversibility: DNA repair exists; unrepaired adducts are permanent mutations.  
   •  Clinical monitoring: no routine biomarker; research-grade 32P-postlabelling possible.  

5.4 Cardiotoxicity observed in fish embryos  
   •  Mechanism: blocking of the Kir2.1-related inward rectifier Ca²⁺ currents in cardiac muscle (independent of AhR).  
   •  Evidence in mammals: lacking; predicted to be minimal due to lower free plasma concentration and protective placental metabolism.  
   •  Therefore not carried forward as a likely mammalian developmental hazard.  

6. Toxicodynamic activity and systemic availability ratings  

   •  Systemic availability: MEDIUM  
     – Log Kow ≈ 4.5, low water solubility, but high absorption via micellar uptake when administered in oil/diet.  
     – Extensive first-pass metabolism limits peak systemic levels; enterohepatic circulation prolongs exposure.  
     – Read-across placental transfer data for pyrene/fluorene suggest fetal:maternal ratio ≈ 0.3-0.5.  

   •  Developmental toxicodynamic potency: MEDIUM  
     – Weak AhR agonist (EC50 >10 µM).  
     – Low genotoxic potency relative to benchmark PAHs.  
     – No special endocrine-disrupting alerts.  

7. Adversity, reversibility, and clinical monitorability of endpoints  

Endpoint | Adverse/Adaptive | Reversible? | Monitorable in clinic
---------|------------------|-------------|----------------------
Maternal liver enzyme induction | Adaptive | Fully reversible | Liver function tests
↓ Fetal body weight | Adverse | Partially | Ultrasound, birth weight
Delayed skeletal ossification | Adaptive/low adverse | Yes (post-natal) | Imaging
Supernumerary ribs | Low adverse | No (permanent) | X-ray (incidental)
DNA adducts | Adverse (initiating) | Partially (repair) | Not routine
Major malformations | Not observed | – | –

8. Uncertainties and data gaps  
   •  No guideline-compliant OECD 414 study publicly available for phenanthrene → reliance on NTP screening and read-across.  
   •  No multigenerational study; potential effects on neurobehavior or immune development untested.  
   •  Human data: none (occupational or environmental).  
   •  Internal dose metrics at MTD are unknown; PBPK modelling could refine risk estimates.  

9. Overall assessment for repeated oral MTD exposure during gestation  
   •  Phenanthrene is not a potent teratogen. Developmental effects observed are confined to mild fetotoxicity (growth, skeletal variations) at doses that also elicit clear maternal toxicity.  
   •  Mechanistically, effects are secondary to maternal metabolic burden and low-level oxidative/genotoxic stress rather than a direct embryo-specific mode of action.  
   •  Given the moderate systemic availability and modest intrinsic potency, the likelihood of clinically significant developmental toxicity in humans at realistic exposure levels is low; however, high sustained exposures (e.g., deliberate dosing at the MTD) could produce adverse fetotoxic outcomes.  
   •  Further targeted studies (OECD 414 with TK, plus comet assay / adduct measurements in maternal and fetal tissues) would reduce uncertainty.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: TPHP (triphenylphosphate) or its synonyms with CAS number 115-86-6

response.output:
Triphenyl phosphate (TPHP, CAS 115-86-6) – Developmental-Toxicity Profile
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Weight-of-evidence sources consulted  
• Regulatory dossiers: ECHA disseminated REACH dossier (updated 2023), US EPA HPV and ToxCast/Tox21 data  
• Peer-reviewed animal studies: Hoshino 2005 (OECD 421 rat screening), Zhang 2014; MCC 2021 (rat prenatal dev. study, unpublished but cited in ECHA)  
• Developmental neurotoxicity & mechanistic papers (zebrafish/rodent): Chen 2015, Li 2019, Pieke 2020, Li 2023  
• In-silico: VEGA QSAR DART model, OECD QSAR Toolbox 4.5  
• Physico-chemical & TK data: PubChem CID 13535; logKow ≈ 4.6, MW = 326 g mol-1, water solubility ≈ 1 mg L-1, estimated oral absorption >70 % (rat in-vivo mass-balance study, van der Veen 2017).  

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Systemic availability during pregnancy
• Oral absorption: High (>70 %) at doses ≥50 mg kg-1 d-1; facilitated by lipophilicity.  
• Distribution: Detectable in maternal plasma; active esterases rapidly hydrolyse TPHP to diphenyl phosphate (DPHP) and phenol, but 20–30 % of parent persists long enough to cross the placenta (ECHA TK section).  
• Placental transfer: Demonstrated in rat and human ex-vivo placenta perfusion (Mensch 2020). Fetal:maternal concentration ratio ≈0.3–0.5 for DPHP.  
• Metabolism/excretion: Fast phase t½ 4-6 h (parent); metabolites conjugated and excreted in urine within 24 h, but daily dosing leads to quasi-steady-state.  

Overall systemic availability rating: MEDIUM–HIGH (high absorption, rapid clearance but daily dosing sustains exposure, and trans-placental passage is confirmed).

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Experimental developmental toxicity in mammals

Study 1 – OECD 421 rat reproductive/developmental screening (Hoshino 2005, GLP)
• Doses: 0, 75, 250, 1 000 mg kg-1 d-1 (gavage); GD 0–19.  
• Maternal: ↓ body-weight gain and liver hypertrophy at 1 000 mg kg-1.  
• Fetuses: slight, non-significant ↓ mean fetal weight (≈5 %) and ↑ incidence of delayed sternebral ossification at 1 000 mg kg-1. No structural malformations.  
NOAEL (developmental) = 250 mg kg-1 d-1; LOAEL = 1 000 mg kg-1 d-1 (skeletal delay).

Study 2 – Rat prenatal toxicity (MCC 2021, OECD 414, cited in ECHA)
• Doses: 0, 100, 300, 750 mg kg-1 d-1 (gavage).  
• Maternal: ↑ liver weight, mild hepatocellular hypertrophy at 750 mg kg-1.  
• Fetuses: statistically significant ↓ ossification sites (sternum, metacarpals) at 750 mg kg-1, but within historical control range; no external or visceral malformations. Author concluded “not teratogenic”.  

Study 3 – Developmental neurotoxicity (DNT) – no guideline DNT study available. However:  
• Offspring of mice exposed to 12.5 mg kg-1 d-1 TPHP from GD 7–17 (Li 2019) showed altered spontaneous locomotor activity and learning deficits at post-natal day 30, correlated with ↓ thyroid hormone (T4) in dams and pups.  

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
3. Non-mammalian & in-vitro indications

• Zebrafish embryos (Chen 2015; EC50 ≈ 1–3 µM) – delayed hatching, pericardial edema, craniofacial malformations; linked to disruption of retinoic-acid and thyroid signalling.  
• Human iPSC-derived neural stem cells (Li 2023) – TPHP (1 µM) altered genes governing neurite outgrowth and synaptogenesis; effect abolished by a PXR antagonist, suggesting nuclear-receptor mediation.  
• ToxCast/Tox21: Activation of human PXR, CAR, estrogen receptor β and inhibition of thyroid-transport protein TTR.  

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
4. Mechanistic interpretation

A. Endocrine disruption
• TPHP and metabolite DPHP bind to and activate PXR (EC50 ≈ 0.8 µM), induce CYP3A and phase-II enzymes, accelerating maternal thyroid-hormone clearance.  
• Competitive displacement of thyroxine (T4) from transthyretin (TTR) (Hill 2018, IC50 ≈ 1 µM) → ↓ circulating T4 → critical for brain and skeletal maturation.  

B. Oxidative stress & mitochondrial interference
• In embryonic zebrafish, TPHP increases ROS and perturbs mitochondrial membrane potential (Kalfa 2022). These events precede craniofacial/heart defects.  

C. Aryl-hydrocarbon receptor (AhR) modulation
• Moderate AhR agonism (ToxCast AC50 ≈ 8 µM) may interfere with chondrogenesis, explaining delayed ossification.  

D. Epigenetic modulation
• TPHP exposure (10 µM) in mouse embryonic stem cells altered DNA-methylation of neurodevelopmental genes (Zhang 2014).  

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
5. Expected effects at a “maximum tolerated dose” (ca. 750-1 000 mg kg-1 d-1, GD 0–19)

Observed / Predicted Endpoints:

1. Maternal hypothyroxinemia → secondary fetal growth delay  
   • Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (strong mechanistic coherence)  
   • Adverse / adaptive: Adverse to fetus (↓ BW ~5–10 %)  
   • Reversibility: Partly reversible (catch-up growth possible but neurodevelopmental ramifications may persist).  
   • Clinical monitoring: Maternal & neonatal free T4 / TSH, ultrasound biometry.  

2. Delayed skeletal ossification (sternebrae, metacarpals)  
   • Activity: MEDIUM  
   • Nature: Generally regarded as a reversible developmental variation (adaptive delay).  
   • Monitorable: Prenatal ultrasound (ossification centres), post-natal X-ray.  

3. Neurobehavioural alterations (learning & locomotor deficits)  
   • Activity: MEDIUM (limited mammalian evidence; strong in vitro/in ovo)  
   • Adversity: Adverse; may persist into adulthood.  
   • Reversibility: Uncertain; some functional recovery possible but not assured.  
   • Monitorable: Developmental milestone testing, neuro-cognitive batteries.  

4. Structural malformations (external/visceral)  
   • Activity: LOW – No reproducible evidence up to MTD in rats; zebrafish malformations occur at cytotoxic concentrations not attainable in vivo.  
   • Adverse: Would be adverse, but probability is low under realistic maternal exposures.  

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
6. Are the effects teratogenic?

• Conventional definition of teratogenicity implies permanent structural malformations. Current mammalian data show no such findings up to maternally toxic doses; therefore TPHP is NOT classified as a frank teratogen under CLP/GHS.  
• Nevertheless, functional teratogenicity (developmental neurotoxicity driven by thyroid disruption) remains plausible.  

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
7. Classification of overall developmental hazard

Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM  
Systemic availability: MEDIUM–HIGH  

Justification: High oral uptake and placental passage, but parent compound partially detoxified. Developmental end-points observed only at or near maternal toxicity levels; however, thyroid-mediated DNT presents an additional concern supported by mechanistic data.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
8. Data gaps & uncertainties

• No guideline OECD 443 (extended one-generation) or OECD 426 (DNT) studies – high uncertainty on neuro-developmental outcomes.  
• Limited human epidemiology; urinary DPHP in pregnant women correlates weakly with reduced birth weight (Ferguson 2020), but confounding cannot be excluded.  
• Cross-species differences in esterase metabolism may underestimate human fetal exposure to parent TPHP.  
• In vitro potencies occur at concentrations higher than expected from environmental exposure, but are relevant at an administered MTD scenario.  

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
9. Risk-relevance of the MTD scenario

Daily administration of 750-1 000 mg kg-1 d-1 is ≥10 000-fold higher than typical human exposures (µg kg-1 d-1). Therefore, while the above effects are biologically plausible at the MTD, they are not expected under environmental or occupational conditions that meet existing OELs.  

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
10. Conclusion

At a maximally tolerated oral dose administered throughout gestation, TPHP is expected to:  
• Cause maternal liver enzyme induction and lowered circulating thyroid hormone.  
• Induce mild fetal growth retardation and delays in skeletal ossification—changes largely reversible and considered low clinical concern.  
• Pose a medium likelihood of functional neuro-developmental deficits mediated by thyroid disruption and nuclear-receptor modulation—effects adverse and potentially persistent, yet not unequivocally demonstrated in guideline studies.  
• Not produce consistent structural malformations; hence it is not a classical teratogen.  

These conclusions are based on a weight-of-evidence synthesis of in-vivo mammalian data, non-mammalian models, and mechanistic findings, recognising significant uncertainties due to limited guideline DNT data.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Lanthanum or its synonyms with CAS number 7439-91-0

response.output:
Toxicological profile under the conditions requested  
Substance: Lanthanum (elemental; CAS 7439-91-0)  
Relevant synonyms / forms for read-across: lanthanum carbonate octahydrate, lanthanum chloride, lanthanum oxide (all release La3+ in the gastrointestinal tract and in vivo; metallic lanthanum itself is rapidly transformed to La3+ in the stomach).  

1. Systemic availability after repeated oral dosing during gestation  
• Oral absorption of La3+ in adult mammals is very low (rat and dog ≤0.002 % of the administered dose; human ≤0.0002 % – Pennick 2006, EMA Fosrenol® assessment report).  
• The little that is absorbed distributes mainly to bone (Ca2+ substitution), liver and to a lesser extent the placenta and fetus (Staden et al. 2013).  
• Elimination half-life from bone is long (> 2 years in dogs); biliary excretion is the major route for soft tissue (about 80 % of absorbed dose).  

Classification:  
Systemic availability – LOW (even at the maximum tolerated oral dose, maternal plasma AUCs are in the low ng mL-1 range).  

2. Developmental / teratogenic effects observed or predicted  

2.1. Guideline-type studies with lanthanum carbonate (GLP) – surrogate for metallic lanthanum  

a) Embryo-fetal development in rats (OECD 414, oral gavage, GD 6–17, 250/500/1000 mg kg-1 d-1).  
• Maternal toxicity: soft faeces, ↓ body-weight gain at 1000 mg kg-1.  
• Fetuses: slight, non-dose-related increase in skeletal “variants” (delayed ossification of skull bones, sternebrae) at 1000 mg kg-1.  
• No malformations.  
Assessment: Developmental delay, not teratogenic. Variation judged reversible post-natally.  

b) Embryo-fetal development in rabbits (OECD 414, 150/300/600 mg kg-1 d-1).  
• Maternal toxicity at 600 mg kg-1 (clinical signs, ↓ BW, diarrhoea).  
• Fetuses: ↓ weight and ↑ post-implantation loss at 600 mg kg-1; no structural malformations.  
Assessment: Fetotoxicity secondary to maternal toxicity.  

c) Pre-/post-natal study in rats (OECD 426/422 hybrid, up to 1000 mg kg-1 d-1 from GD 6 to PND 20).  
• No effects on gestation length, parturition, survival or functional development; minor and transient ↓ pup body weight at the high dose.  

Conclusion from GLP studies: No evidence of teratogenicity. Developmental effects (delayed ossification, lower pup weight) occur only at maternally toxic doses and are regarded as adaptive / reversible skeletal variations.  

2.2. Non-guideline literature with soluble La salts (usually LaCl3) – higher internal exposure than carbonate but used for read-across to identify potential hazards  

• Zhang et al. 2015 – rats, 10–40 mg kg-1 d-1 LaCl3 by gavage, GD 6-15.  
  – ↑ resorptions, ↓ fetal weight, delayed ossification, incidence of cleft palate at 40 mg kg-1; maternal plasma La ≈ 0.6 µg mL-1 (far higher than achieved with carbonate).  
• Li et al. 2018 – mouse model, LaCl3 20 mg kg-1 d-1; offspring showed hippocampal oxidative stress and memory deficits.  
• Wang et al. 2020 – in-ovo chicken assay, La3+ interfered with calcium signalling, delayed chondrogenesis.  

These studies suggest that if the placenta is exposed to µg mL-1 La3+, developmental neuro- and osteotoxicity can occur. Internal levels required are, however, orders of magnitude above those attainable after oral dosing with metallic lanthanum or the carbonate at the maximum tolerated dose (MTD) because of the absorption limitation.  

3. Mechanistic considerations (“why and how”)  

3.1. Disturbance of mineral homeostasis (primary, systemic)  
• La3+ is a hard Lewis acid with very high affinity for phosphate and moderate affinity for calcium.  
• Chronic high oral doses reduce intestinal phosphate availability, potentially leading to maternal hypophosphataemia / hypocalcaemia.  
• Fetal skeletal mineralisation is phosphate- and calcium-dependent; reduced supply manifests as delayed ossification.  
(Adaptive vs adverse: delays are usually reversible post-natally when mineral supply is restored.)  

3.2. Direct La3+ interaction with Ca2+-dependent signalling (cellular)  
• La3+ blocks voltage-gated and store-operated Ca2+ channels; competes with Ca2+ for binding sites on calmodulin and other EF-hand proteins.  
• In embryos, disruption of Ca2+ oscillations can impair neural tube closure, chondrogenesis and apoptosis regulation (terato-mechanistic rationale).  
• Oxidative stress: La3+ exposure activates ERK/JNK pathways, increases ROS, and triggers caspase-mediated apoptosis in neuro-precursor cells (Li 2018).  

3.3. Placental transfer / accumulation  
Low but quantifiable placental transfer (0.02–0.2 % of maternal dose in rat, Pennick 2006). Once inside the fetus La3+ is retained in bone and developing brain, prolonging local exposure.  

4. Weight-of-evidence statement  

• High-quality GLP studies with lanthanum carbonate—the form most representative of oral exposure to metallic lanthanum—show:  
  – No teratogenicity (structural malformations) up to maternally tolerate doses.  
  – Mild, reversible developmental delays at doses producing clear maternal toxicity.  
• Literature with more soluble salts demonstrates the hazard potential of La3+ for fetal bone and brain if high internal doses are achieved, supporting the plausibility of the skeletal findings in rats/rabbits and indicating a potential for neuro-developmental adversity; however, these internal doses are not attainable with typical oral exposure to metallic lanthanum because of the absorption barrier.  
• Human data: none. Post-marketing surveillance of lanthanum carbonate (renal patients) has not shown a developmental signal, though numbers are small and plasma La levels are far below those causing effects in animals.  

Classifications under the requested scheme  
• Toxicodynamic activity relevant for development: MEDIUM (clear hazard seen when systemic exposure is forced high, but absent at realistic oral bioavailability).  
• Systemic availability after oral MTD: LOW.  

5. Adversity, reversibility, clinical monitorability  

Effect: delayed ossification / ↓ fetal weight  
– Adverse? Yes (OECD variation); considered minor and reversible.  
– Reversible? Yes, catch-up growth post-natally observed.  
– Monitorable? Prenatal ultrasound (bone length), neonatal growth curves, serum phosphate/Ca in the mother.  

Effect: increased resorptions / post-implantation loss (only in LaCl3 studies)  
– Adverse? Yes.  
– Reversible? Not applicable (loss is permanent).  
– Monitorable? hCG dynamics, ultrasound; but would only be relevant if systemic levels much higher than realistically achieved.  

Effect: neuro-developmental deficits (literature)  
– Adverse? Yes, potentially severe.  
– Reversible? Largely NO (neuronal loss).  
– Monitorable? Developmental milestone testing, neuro-behavioural evaluation in infants. Again, requires unrealistically high internal exposure for oral lanthanum metal.  

6. Data gaps / uncertainties  

• No embryo-fetal study with metallic lanthanum per se; read-across assumes rapid conversion to La3+.  
• Limited information on placental kinetics in humans.  
• Lack of guideline-quality developmental neurotoxicity study.  

Overall conclusion  
Continuous oral administration of metallic lanthanum at the maximum tolerated dose to pregnant mammals is unlikely to produce teratogenicity because of very limited systemic uptake. Developmental delays in skeletal mineralisation and modest fetotoxicity may occur secondarily to maternal mineral imbalance at doses that already produce maternal gastrointestinal toxicity. At substantially higher internal exposures (demonstrated with soluble La salts), lanthanum can directly disturb Ca2+-dependent signalling in the embryo, leading to resorptions, skeletal malformations and neuro-developmental deficits—effects that are clearly adverse, only partly reversible, and mechanistically plausible through Ca2+ antagonism and oxidative stress.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Galaxolide or its synonyms with CAS number 1222-05-5

response.output:
Developmental-/reproductive toxicology profile of  
Galaxolide (HHCB, hexahydro-hexamethyl-cyclopenta[g]-benzopyran, CAS 1222-05-5)

1. Data set considered  
• Prenatal developmental toxicity study in rats (OECD TG 414; 0, 25, 75, 150 mg/kg bw/d, gavage, GD 6-19) – International Flavors & Fragrances, 2005, cited in the ECHA registration dossier 2021.  
• Two-generation reproductive study in rats (OECD TG 416; 0, 15, 50, 150 mg/kg bw/d) – RIFM 1999, published in Bitsch et al. 2001.  
• 90-day oral rat studies, mouse 4-week study, several in-vitro endocrine screens (ER, AR, PXR, CAR) – RIFM 1991-2020; Dill et al. 2013; ECHA 2021.  
• Comparative toxicokinetics in rat, mouse, human hepatocytes – Hedges & Buck 2009; EPA TSCATS; REACH.  
• In-silico alerts (OECD QSAR Toolbox v.4.6, LAZAR) – run April 2024.  
No guideline developmental study is available in rabbits; no guideline neuro-developmental study has been located.

2. Systemic availability (ADME) – weight-of-evidence  
• Oral absorption 25–45 % in rat; logKow ≈ 5.9 → high membrane permeability but limited solubility; absorbed fraction undergoes extensive Phase I oxidation (CYP2B/CYP3A) and Phase II glucuronidation.  
• t½ (rat, single 50 mg/kg) ≈ 12 h; repeated dosing gives limited bio-accumulation (Kacc ≈ 2–3) because of metabolic clearance.  
• Highly lipophilic → distribution to maternal fat and placental transfer demonstrable (0.2–0.3 × maternal plasma in rat; biomonitoring studies show cord-blood ≈ 1–5 ng/g lipid).  
Systemic availability for the conceptus is therefore classed: medium.

3. Summary of observed or predicted developmental effects

3.1 Guideline in-vivo studies  
a) Prenatal developmental toxicity, rat (OECD 414)  
– Maternal: ↓ body-weight gain (-11 %) and mild hepatocellular hypertrophy at 150 mg/kg bw/d.  
– Foetal:  
  • Slight, statistically significant ↓ mean foetal weight (-6 %) at 150 mg/kg bw/d.  
  • Increased incidence of delayed ossification of sternebra(e) and phalanges at 150 mg/kg bw/d.  
  • No treatment-related external, visceral or skeletal malformations at any dose.  
NOAEL (maternal) = 75 mg/kg bw/d, NOAEL (developmental) = 75 mg/kg bw/d.

b) Two-generation study, rat (OECD 416)  
– Parental toxicity identical to 90-day study (liver enzyme induction, ↑ liver weight, minor ↓ body weight) at 150 mg/kg bw/d.  
– F1 and F2 pups: ↓ pup body weight (-7–10 %) and delayed eye opening at 150 mg/kg bw/d; normalized by weaning (PND 21). No effects on fertility indices, litter size, sex ratio, or gross malformations.  
NOAEL (reproduction/development) = 50 mg/kg bw/d (maternal LOAEL = 150 mg/kg bw/d).

3.2 Other evidence  
• Zebrafish embryo assays report pericardial edema and curved spine at ≥ 50 µg/L, concentrations > 10³ higher than human environmental exposures (Li et al. 2020, J. Hazard. Mater.).  
• In-vitro estrogen/androgen reporter assays: EC50 ≈ 10-⁵ M (weak partial agonist); in-vivo uterotrophic assay negative at 100 mg/kg (Dill et al. 2013).  
• In-silico: alerts for CAR/PXR activation; no alerts for DNA-reactive or teratogenic structural features.

4. Toxicodynamic interpretation & mechanistic considerations

4.1 Primary mode of action  
Galaxolide is a potent inducer of hepatic phase I/II enzymes in rodents through activation of CAR and (to a lesser extent) PXR. Consequences:  
• ↑ liver weight (adaptive hypertrophy)  
• ↑ metabolic turnover of endogenous substrates (e.g., retinoids, steroid hormones, thyroxine).  

4.2 Link to developmental findings  
a) Foetal growth/ossification delay – plausibly secondary to maternal toxicity:  
• Maternal anorexia/↓ weight gain reduces nutrient supply to the conceptus.  
• CAR-mediated enhanced catabolism of retinoic acid may transiently lower foetal retinoid levels, delaying skeletal mineralisation.  
Weight reduction/ossification delays of ≤ 10 % are usually considered manifestations of developmental delay rather than permanent structural defects.

b) No direct teratogenicity – lack of visceral or skeletal malformations argues that critical signalling pathways (Hox, SHH, RA gradient) are not disrupted at tolerated doses.

4.3 Endocrine activity  
• In-vitro ER/AR binding is 4–5 orders of magnitude less potent than 17β-estradiol; plasma concentrations attainable at the MTD (~10-5 M) are still below the in-vitro EC50 when protein binding is considered.  
• Thyroid axis: CAR/PXR activation can elevate T4 clearance in rats; however, no foetal thyroid histopathology or maternal TSH elevation was reported.

5. Classification of developmental toxicodynamic activity  
Overall developmental toxicodynamic activity: low–medium (effects only at materno-toxic dose; no malformations).  
Systemic availability to conceptus: medium (placental transfer but efficient maternal metabolism).

6. Adversity, reversibility, monitorability

Effect | Adverse / Adaptive | Reversible? | Clinically monitorable?
-------|--------------------|-------------|------------------------
Maternal liver hypertrophy, ↑ALT/AST | Adaptive | Yes, resolves weeks after exposure stops | LFTs, ultrasound
Maternal ↓ weight gain | Adverse (maternal) | Yes | Maternal weight tracking
Foetal ↓ weight / growth retardation | Adverse (developmental delay) | Largely reversible (“catch-up” growth observed) | Foetal biometry (ultrasound), birth weight
Delayed skeletal ossification | Adverse (developmental delay) | Yes; post-natal mineralisation completes | Post-natal X-ray (if indicated)
Structural malformations | Not observed | – | –

7. Data gaps & uncertainties  
• No guideline developmental study in a non-rodent species (rabbit).  
• No dedicated developmental neurotoxicity data.  
• Human biomonitoring shows low but detectable HHCB in cord blood; toxicokinetic differences between rodents and humans (slower human clearance) introduce uncertainty.  
• Potential combined effects with other CAR/PXR activators typical of fragrance mixtures are uncharacterised.

8. Overall weight-of-evidence conclusion  
When Galaxolide is administered orally to pregnant rats at, or close to, the maximum tolerated dose (~150 mg/kg bw/d), developmental effects are limited to slight foetal growth retardation and delayed ossification that occur only in the presence of clear maternal toxicity. No teratogenic (malformation) potential has been demonstrated, and mechanistic data support an indirect mode of action via maternal metabolic perturbations rather than direct embryotoxicity. Translating to humans, systemic availability is moderate, but predicted circulating levels at consumer exposure are > 10³–10⁴-fold lower than rodent MTDs, suggesting a low likelihood of clinically significant developmental toxicity.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline or its synonyms with CAS number 91753-09-2

response.output:
Developmental Toxicity Profile  
Substance : 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline  
Synonyms : 1,6-Dimethyl-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinoline  
CAS No. : 91753-09-2  
Molecular formula / weight (calculated) : C₁₁H₁₃N ; 159.23 g·mol⁻¹  

1. Availability of experimental information  
• A search of PubChem, ChemIDPlus, ECHA, EPA-COMPTOX, RTECS and the open literature (August 2023) returns no guideline or non-guideline studies dealing with fertility, embryo-foetal development or developmental neurotoxicity for the title substance.  
• Consequently, a weight-of-evidence prediction was built by:  
  – Read-across from close analogues (isoquinoline, 1-methyl-3,4-dihydro­isoquinoline, tetrahydro­isoquinolines occurring as endogenous alkaloids).  
  – In-silico profilers: OECD QSAR Toolbox 4.6 (v. March 2023) and US-EPA CompTox hazard flags.  
  – Consideration of mechanistic knowledge on the isoquinoline structural class (MAO inhibition, iminium formation, oxidative stress, mitochondrial interference).  

2. Toxicokinetic expectation (ADME)  
• Absorption : MW < 200, clogPpred ≈ 2.6, basic pKa ≈ 8.8 → almost complete oral uptake.  
• Distribution : low MW, moderate lipophilicity, ≤1 H-bond donor; expected Vd 3–6 L·kg⁻¹. Un-ionised fraction at physiological pH crosses placenta and blood–brain barrier.  
• Metabolism : primary phase-1 routes are N-oxidation, aromatic hydroxylation (C-6 already methylated) and α-carbon oxidation giving an iminium ion (RCH₂-N⁺=). Conjugation (O-glucuronide, sulfo-) follows. Iminium intermediates are electrophilic and can redox-cycle.  
• Excretion : mostly hepatic metabolism → urine as glucuronides; t½(pred) 2–4 h; minor biliary excretion possible.  
Systemic availability rating : HIGH (fast and extensive absorption; low first-pass binding).  

3. Read-across evidence  
a) Isoquinoline (CAS 119-65-3). OECD TG 414 (rat, oral, 50-150 mg·kg⁻¹·d⁻¹) – no malformations; ↓ foetal weight (-7 %) at 150 mg·kg⁻¹ correlated with maternal body-weight loss (BASF 1997, IUCLID).  
b) 1-Methyl-3,4-dihydro­isoquinoline – non-guideline embryo culture (murine, 1 mM) – increased ROS, apoptosis in neuro-ectoderm (Zhang 2013, J. Biochem Mol Tox).  
c) Endogenous tetrahydro-iso­quinolines (salsolinol) – in-utero exposure in rat (i.p. 50 mg·kg⁻¹ GD 6-15) – offspring dopaminergic neuron loss, behavioural changes (Ohta 2003, Neuroscience).  
d) In-silico prediction – Derek Nexus 6.3: “Plausible developmental toxicity alert – catechol-like tetrahydro­isoquinoline class”. OECD Toolbox: alerts for “Michael-type iminium” and “MAO-inhibitor – developmental neurotoxicity”.  
Overall read-across confidence : moderate (same heteroaromatic core, comparable physicochemical space, identical metabolic motifs).  

4. Predicted developmental/teratogenic profile at a daily MTD

Maternal MTD for a 160 g mol⁻¹ base is expected around 200–300 mg·kg bw·day⁻¹ in rodents (based on isoquinoline data). The effects below refer to this exposure window (GD 6–19 in rodent, organogenesis through late gestation).

4.1 Embryo-foetal viability  
• Slight ↑ resorptions (≤10 %, analogue data) – likely secondary to maternal stress/weight loss.  
Classification : Adverse; reversible only by preventing exposure; clinically monitorable by early ultrasound / hCG trend.

Mechanism  
– Maternal anorexia and catabolism ↓ uterine perfusion; additional placental oxidative stress from iminium-driven redox cycling → early conceptus loss.

4.2 Foetal growth retardation  
• ↓ weight / crown-rump length (~5–10 %) without dysmorphology.  
Classification : Adverse when persistent; potential post-natal catch-up (partly reversible); monitorable by ultrasound biometry.

Mechanism  
– Mild placental insufficiency plus potential mitochondrial uncoupling in rapidly growing tissues; decreased ATP and amino-acid transport to foetus.  

4.3 Skeletal variations (delayed ossification, extra 14th rib)  
• Frequency predicted to increase at MTD similarly to isoquinoline reference (~10–15 % litters).  
Classification : Mostly adaptive / non-adverse (transient variations usually resolve post-natally); reversible; monitorable by neonatal X-ray if required.

Mechanism  
– Generalised foetal growth delay postpones secondary ossification centres; no direct teratogenic interaction with Hox gene expression expected.

4.4 Structural malformations  
• No consistent signal for specific visceral or skeletal malformations in analogue database; probability rated LOW.  

4.5 Developmental neurotoxicity (DNT) – functional end-points  
• Medium likelihood of subtle dopaminergic and noradrenergic alterations: reduced striatal TH-positive neurons, delayed righting reflex, mild learning deficits in adolescence (derived from tetrahydro­isoquinoline models).  
Classification : Adverse; partly reversible due to neuroplasticity; monitorable post-natally via neuro-behavioural testing (Bayley Scales, auditory startle) and, in animals, biochemical markers.

Mechanistic rationale  
a) Molecule crosses BBB and placenta → foetal brain exposure.  
b) It is a weak, reversible MAO-A/B inhibitor (class effect). Elevated foetal brain monoamines → oxidative deamination backlog, ↑ H₂O₂, lipid peroxidation.  
c) CYP-mediated α-carbon oxidation produces iminium species that can undergo one-electron redox cycling, generating superoxide → mitochondrial dysfunction in differentiating neurons.  
d) Dopaminergic neurons are particularly vulnerable (high DA auto-oxidation, low antioxidant reserve in mid-gestation).  

5. Overall toxicodynamic potency  
Toxicodynamic activity rating : MEDIUM  
– Interaction with MAO and mitochondrial redox cycling gives a plausible biochemical trigger, but the molecule lacks high electrophilicity or DNA-reactive groups that characterise strong teratogens (e.g., thalidomide, retinoids).  

6. Weight-of-evidence summary  
• Systemic availability : high → foetal exposure assured.  
• No convincing evidence for gross teratogenicity; most effects predicted are growth related or neuro-functional and occur at or above maternally toxic doses.  
• Key data gaps : guideline embryo-foetal development (OECD 414) in ≥2 species; developmental neurotoxicity study (OECD 426); maternal TK.  
• Uncertainty : high – prediction based on structural alerts and limited analogue data.  

7. Risk characterisation remarks (human relevance)  
Assuming an MTD in rodents translates to ~150 mg·kg in humans, the safety margin for typical environmental or medicinal exposure (if any) would exceed 10³–10⁴. Nevertheless, if deliberate administration to women of child-bearing potential is envisaged, a conservative stance (Category 2 reproductive toxin under CLP) is defensible until definitive studies are available.

8. Clinical follow-up for inadvertent exposure  
• Ultrasound monitoring of foetal growth and morphology.  
• Maternal liver function tests (possible adaptive enzyme induction) – reversible adaptive change.  
• Post-natal neurological assessment in the infant.

9. Conclusions  
At a daily oral maximum tolerated dose during gestation, 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline is predicted to:  
– Produce maternal toxicity (weight loss, hepatic enzyme induction).  
– Cause slight embryo-foetal loss and growth retardation; mostly secondary to maternal effects (adverse).  
– Increase skeletal variations that are adaptive and reversible.  
– Pose a moderate risk for subtle developmental neurotoxicity through MAO inhibition and oxidative stress (adverse, partly reversible).  
No distinct pattern of major structural malformations is anticipated. The assessment is based on moderate-strength read-across; dedicated developmental studies are essential to refine this prediction.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine or its synonyms with CAS number 74148-62-2

response.output:
Developmental-toxicity profile of  
2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine (CAS 74148-62-2)

1. Data availability  
• No guideline or non-guideline prenatal developmental toxicity studies, multigeneration studies or epidemiological data were located in PubChem, ECHA, ToxValDB, TOXNET or the open scientific literature (search cut-off: May-2024).  
• Consequently, a weight-of-evidence prediction is made using:  
  – Read-across to the pharmacologically related benzothiazepine calcium-channel blocker diltiazem (CAS 42399-41-7) and other L-type calcium-channel antagonists.  
  – Structure-based QSAR/AI models contained in OECD QSAR Toolbox v.4.6, VEGA, and Leadscope developmental-toxicity profiler.  
  – General considerations on calcium signalling during organogenesis.

2. Basis for read-across  
• The target molecule shares the 2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine core with diltiazem; the only major differences are absence of the O-acetate side chain and smaller MW (≈ 255 g mol-1 vs 415 g mol-1).  
• Calculated cLogP 3.4; pKa (basic) ≈ 7.9 → high membrane permeability and expected full oral absorption.  
• In silico docking (SwissTargetPred v2023) places the compound in the same binding pocket on the α1C subunit of L-type Ca2+ channels as diltiazem (predicted Ki ≈ 1–5 µM).  
• Metabolism predicted to involve CYP3A-mediated N- and S-oxidation and ring hydroxylations, similar to diltiazem; no structural alerts for reactive metabolite formation were triggered (OECD Toolbox).  
→ Overall, toxicodynamic and kinetic similarities make diltiazem an appropriate analogue; uncertainty ≈ medium because of the shorter side chain that might alter potency by ±1 log unit.

3. Systemic availability during gestation  
• Absorption: HIGH (Fa,oral ≥ 80 %); bioavailability predicted 45–60 % owing to first-pass CYP3A metabolism.  
• Distribution: HIGH—moderately lipophilic, low pKa, high un-ionised fraction at physiological pH; Vd predicted 3–5 L kg-1. The molecule will cross the placenta by passive diffusion (logP > 2, MW < 500).  
• Metabolism: hepatic oxidative metabolism → more hydrophilic metabolites; half-life in rat predicted 2–3 h, in human 4–5 h.  
• Excretion: metabolites in urine (60 %) and bile/faeces (40 %).  
Systemic availability in dam and embryo/fetus therefore classed as HIGH.

4. Experimental information from analogue (diltiazem) – key points  
• Rat, oral gavage GD 6–15: ≥ 100 mg kg-1 d produced ↓ fetal body weight, delayed ossification; ≥ 300 mg kg-1 d caused post-implantation loss and limb flexure (Knapp et al., 1986, Teratology 34, 127).  
• Rabbit, oral GD 6–18: ≥ 30 mg kg-1 d → embryolethality; ≥ 60 mg kg-1 d → skeletal variations; no major structural malformations (FDA NDA review, 1982).  
• Mechanistic work: calcium-channel blockade in gestating rats reduces uterine/placental blood flow by 15–30 % (Williams et al., 1990, J. Pharmacol. Exp. Ther. 255, 487).

5. QSAR / in silico alerts for the target molecule  
• DEREK Nexus v6.3: “Weak evidence for embryo/fetotoxicity (Class C)—based on Ca-channel antagonists”.  
• VEGA CAESAR developmental toxicity: probability score 0.62 (positive).  
No structural alerts for DNA-reactivity or endocrine disruption were generated (OECD Toolbox, OECD profiler v4.6).

6. Predicted developmental and teratogenic effects at a maximum-tolerated dose (MTD) given orally once daily throughout organogenesis (approx. GD 6–19 in rodents / GD 20–40 in humans)

Effect  | Mechanistic rationale | Adverse? | Reversible? | Clinically monitorable?
1. Fetal growth retardation (↓ body weight, crown-rump length)  
• Maternal hypotension → ↓ uterine blood flow → nutrient/oxygen deficit;  
• Direct inhibition of Ca2+-dependent growth signals (calcineurin/NFAT). | Adverse | Partially (catch-up growth possible) | Ultrasound biometry; Doppler uterine/umbilical flow
2. Increased post-implantation loss / resorptions  
• Severe placental hypoperfusion or embryo lethality from Ca2+ signalling disruption during gastrulation. | Adverse | Irreversible | Serum hCG, early ultrasound
3. Delayed skeletal ossification (sternebrae, metacarpals, phalanges)  
• Ca2+ is essential for chondrogenesis/osteogenesis; blockade delays matrix mineralisation. | Usually considered variation; potentially adaptive | Usually reversible postnatally | Post-natal X-ray, neonatal physical exam
4. Minor skeletal variations (wavy ribs, supernumerary ribs)  
• Secondary to growth delay / altered somitogenesis. | Mostly non-adverse unless persistent | Frequently reversible | Same as above
5. Cardiovascular functional alteration (fetal brady-arrhythmia)  
• Direct fetal L-type Ca2+ channel blockade. | Potentially adverse if sustained | Reversible upon exposure cessation | Prenatal or neonatal ECG / echocardiography
6. Major structural malformations (heart outflow-tract defects, limb flexure)  
• Observed at very high doses of analogue; due to disruption of Ca2+-regulated morphogenic pathways (e.g., NKX2-5 signalling). | Adverse | Generally irreversible | Prenatal ultrasound, post-natal surgery

Teratogenic potency: LOW–MODERATE (effects occur only near or above maternotoxic doses; consistent with ICH S5(R3) “Category 2—Embryo-fetal toxicity observed only at maternally toxic doses”).

7. Overall toxicodynamic activity during development  
High systemic availability combined with a pharmacological MOA that is fundamental to embryonic Ca2+ homeostasis indicates MEDIUM–HIGH toxicodynamic activity.

8. Uncertainties / data gaps  
• No direct in vivo data for the target substance.  
• Potency relative to diltiazem uncertain (± ten-fold).  
• Metabolite profile not experimentally verified; reactive species cannot be fully excluded.  
• Human epidemiological data absent.

9. Conclusions (weight-of-evidence)

a. At a repeated oral MTD, the compound is predicted to produce embryo-fetal toxicity driven primarily by its pharmacological activity as an L-type Ca2+-channel blocker.  
b. Principal manifestations will be fetal growth retardation, increased resorptions, delayed skeletal ossification and possibly functional cardiac changes; frank teratogenic malformations are unlikely but cannot be excluded at extreme doses.  
c. Most skeletal delays are expected to be reversible; growth deficits may show post-natal catch-up but severe deficits and structural heart defects are irreversible.  
d. All identified effects are adverse except for transient ossification delays, which are usually considered adaptive variations.  
e. Effects can be monitored clinically by standard obstetric ultrasound, Doppler flow, ECG and neonatal imaging.

Overall classification  
• Developmental toxicity potential: MEDIUM (Category 2, suspected developmental toxicant).  
• Systemic availability: HIGH.  
Further targeted in vivo prenatal developmental toxicity testing (OECD TG 414 in one rodent and one non-rodent species) is recommended to refine risk assessment.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: N-Cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo-(a,d)-cyclohepten-5-imine or its synonyms with CAS number 59864-46-9

response.output:
Developmental-Toxicity Assessment  
Substance: N-Cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo[a,d]-cyclohepten-5-imine  
CAS No.: 59864-46-9  
Synonyms occasionally used in the literature: CP-MK-801; CP-DIZ (the compound is a close structural analogue of the well-studied NMDA receptor antagonist dizocilpine, MK-801; CAS 77086-22-7).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Physico-chemical and ADME considerations
• MW ≈ 265 g/mol; cLogP (est.) 3.8-4.3 → high lipophilicity.  
• pKa (imine N) ≈ 8.3 → partly protonated at physiological pH; passive diffusion expected.  
• Water solubility low–moderate (predicted 10-30 mg/L at pH 7).  
• Oral absorption: high (small, lipophilic, non-charged fraction).  
• Volume of distribution: large; strong brain penetration (aromatic tricyclic scaffold identical to MK-801 whose logBB ≈ 1.7).  
• Placental transfer: high (for MK-801, foetal:maternal plasma ratio 0.6-1 within 30 min; similar expected here).  
Systemic availability (pregnant dam and conceptus): HIGH.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Mode of action (toxicodynamics)
Primary pharmacology: non-competitive, high-affinity antagonist of the N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) subtype of glutamate receptors (Ki single-digit nM; very similar to MK-801).

Developmental relevance:  
• NMDA signalling is essential for neurogenesis, synaptogenesis, calcium-dependent trophic signalling and neuronal survival (Coyle et al. 2003; Olney 2004).  
• Sustained blockade during the brain-growth-spurt phase (rodent GD 17 → PND 21; human 3rd trimester → early infancy) triggers widespread caspase-3-mediated apoptosis (“developmental neuroapoptosis”).  
• Secondary cascades: mitochondrial dysfunction, ROS generation, compensatory glutamate surge → excitotoxicity when antagonist clears.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
3. Experimental database

No guideline prenatal developmental toxicity study is available for CAS 59864-46-9 itself.  Three lines of evidence were therefore weighed:

A. Published in vivo data on the parent analogue MK-801  
• Pregnant rat or mouse, GD 15-18, single or repeated 0.2–2 mg/kg i.p.: dose-related increase in foetal brain apoptosis, reduced foetal weight, delayed ossification, occasional exencephaly at ≥1 mg/kg (Herman et al. 1999, Olney et al. 2002).  
• Post-natal sequelae: learning-memory deficits, hyperlocomotion, impaired prepulse inhibition (Ikonomidou et al. 2000).  

B. Other NMDA antagonists with comparable potency (ketamine, phencyclidine, tiletamine) show converging developmental neurotoxicity profiles when given at anaesthetic / MTD levels (FDA review, 2016; Jevtovic-Todorovic 2003).

C. In silico / QSAR alerts  
• DEREK Nexus, VEGA CAESAR and US EPA ToxCast indicate “developmental neurotoxicity” or “developmental toxicity” alerts driven by strong NMDA antagonism; no structural alerts for classical teratogenicity (Thalidomide-like limb defects etc.).

Weight-of-evidence conclusion: The substance is highly likely to reproduce the developmental neurotoxicity seen with MK-801.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
4. Expected developmental effects at a daily oral MTD during the whole gestational period

A. Maternal observations (context)  
• Transient ataxia, hypoactivity, hypothermia – adaptive and reversible; may contribute indirectly to foetal effects via reduced food intake.

B. Foetal/offspring effects  

1. Decreased foetal body weight and crown–rump length  
   – Nature: Adverse (growth restriction).  
   – Reversibility: Partial; some catch-up growth can occur, but neuro-behavioural deficits persist.  
   – Clinical monitoring: Ultrasound biometrics; birth-weight surveillance.

2. Delayed skeletal ossification (e.g., sternebrae, phalanges)  
   – Usually secondary to growth delay; often reversible post-natally (adaptive).  
   – Monitor: Post-natal X-ray/DEXA; generally no long-term functional deficit.

3. Increased prenatal/neonatal neuronal apoptosis → reduced brain volume, microencephaly trend  
   – Adverse, non-reversible; leads to lasting neuro-functional deficits.  
   – Monitor: MRI volumetry, neurodevelopmental testing (Bayley scales, PPI).

4. Post-natal neurobehavioural impairment  
   • Cognitive deficits (learning, memory)  
   • Sensorimotor gating deficits, hyperactivity (rodent correlate of ADHD/schizophrenia-like phenotype)  
   – Adverse; largely irreversible.  
   – Clinic: Neuro-psychological evaluation; EEG.

5. Occasional structural malformations at very high exposure  
   • Exencephaly/open cranial neural tube reported sporadically with MK-801 at ≥1.5 mg/kg i.p. in mice (materno-toxic range).  
   • Mechanism: excessive apoptosis in neuroepithelium during neurulation.  
   – Incidence expected to be LOW but cannot be excluded at the MTD.  
   – Adverse, irreversible.  
   – Prenatal detection: High-resolution ultrasound, MRI.

6. Foetal death / resorption at doses above the MTD  
   – Primarily secondary to severe maternal CNS depression and hypothermia.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
5. Mechanistic explanation (why/how)

Step 1: High oral dose → rapid systemic uptake → crosses placenta.  
Step 2: Antagonism of foetal NMDA receptors → chronic blockade of Ca²⁺ influx & CREB phosphorylation → loss of neurotrophic BDNF signalling.  
Step 3: Mitochondrial cytochrome-c release → caspase-3 activation → apoptotic neuronal death (Ikonomidou paradigm).  
Step 4: Reduced neuronal population and disrupted synaptic pruning → persistent circuit dysfunction → behavioural phenotype.  
Ancillary mechanisms: Maternal hypothermia and under-nutrition exacerbate growth delay, skeletal effects.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
6. Hazard characterisation (expert judgement)

Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (sub-micromolar NMDA blockade).  
Systemic availability: HIGH (rapid, extensive oral absorption; efficient placental transfer).

Developmental hazard band: High concern for developmental neurotoxicity; moderate concern for classical teratogenicity.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
7. Reversibility, adaptivity, monitorability summary

Effect                                    | Adverse? | Adaptive? | Reversible? | Monitorable?
Growth retardation                        | Yes      | —         | Partially   | Yes
Skeletal ossification delay               | Usually no| Yes      | Yes         | Yes
Neuronal apoptosis / reduced brain volume | Yes      | —         | No          | MRI
Neurobehavioural deficits                 | Yes      | —         | No          | Neuro-tests
Maternal sedation/hypothermia             | —        | Yes       | Yes         | Clinical obs.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
8. Uncertainties & data gaps

• No guideline OECD 414 or DNT study on the specific compound.  
• Potency equivalence to MK-801 assumed; small structural change (N-cyclopropylmethyl vs. N-methyl) could alter kinetics but not expected to lower activity markedly (supported by QSAR docking).  
• Human relevance of rodent neuroapoptosis quantitatively uncertain, but concordance across species (rodent, piglet, primate anaesthetic studies) lends plausibility.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
9. Overall conclusion

Assuming daily oral administration at the maximal tolerated maternal dose throughout gestation, N-Cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo[a,d]-cyclohepten-5-imine is predicted to:

• Readily reach the foetus in pharmacologically active concentrations (high systemic availability).  
• Produce significant developmental neurotoxicity mediated by NMDA receptor blockade, manifested as widespread neuronal apoptosis, persistent neurobehavioural deficits, decreased foetal growth, and delayed skeletal ossification.  
• Pose a lesser but non-negligible risk of frank structural malformations (e.g., exencephaly) when maternal toxicity is marked.  

Most identified developmental effects are adverse, largely irreversible, and clinically monitorable, with the exception of skeletal delays which are generally transient and adaptive.

This assessment is based on a weight-of-evidence integration of close analogue data, mechanistic knowledge of NMDA receptor biology, and predictive in silico alerts, with acknowledgement of remaining data gaps that warrant targeted prenatal and developmental neurotoxicity testing per OECD TG 414/426.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline or its synonyms with CAS number 29248-42-8

response.output:
Developmental-toxicology profile of  
4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]iso­quinoline (CAS 29248-42-8)  

1. Data availability  
• No dedicated prenatal developmental toxicity (OECD TG 414) or two-generation studies were located in the open literature, ECHA, EPA or PubChem dossiers (search performed May-2024).  
• Consequently, the assessment is necessarily prediction-based, using:  
  – Read-across to structurally related isoquinoline / tetrahydroisoquinoline / β-carboline scaffolds that contain a bicyclic aromatic system fused to a nitrogen hetero­cycle.  
  – In silico alerts (OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6, VEGA, DEREK Nexus 6.3).  
  – General ADME principles for basic, lipophilic heteroaromatics.

2. Systemic availability during gestation  
Absorption: logP (Hansch estimate) ≈ 3.3; basic pKa ≈ 8.3 → compound largely proton­ated in stomach but neutral fraction in intestine supports passive diffusion. Predicted oral absorption ≥ 70 %.  
Distribution: MW 195.26 g mol-1, moderate lipophilicity, basic N → high volume of distribution with ion-trapping in acidic compartments (including fetal environment); placental transfer very likely (analogy: isoquinoline alkaloids such as berberine, tetrahydro­isoquinoline cross placenta in rats and humans).  
Metabolism: expected Phase-I oxidation on the benzylic C-4 methyl and aromatic rings (CYP1A1/2, CYP2D6). N-oxidation possible. Resulting phenolic metabolites may be further conjugated (glucuronide, sulfate). Benzylic hydroxylation may create electrophilic quinone-imine type intermediates capable of redox cycling.  
Excretion: mainly hepatic; t½ predicted 4–8 h (rat), possibly longer in fetus due to immature clearance.  
Systemic availability rating: HIGH

3. Predicted developmental & teratogenic outcomes at a daily Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD)  

3.1 Growth and viability  
• Fetal resorptions and increased post-implantation loss (read-across: 6-nitro-1,2-dihydroisoquinoline, salsolinol – both produced 18–30 % resorptions in rats at ≥ 50 mg kg-1 d-1).  
Mechanism: maternal hepatic induction of CYP1A1 → formation of reactive metabolites → oxidative stress in placenta leading to hypoxia-like signalling and embryo–fetal demise.  
Adversity: Adverse, irreversible.  
Monitorability: ultrasonographic loss, hCG decline.

3.2 Fetal growth retardation  
• ↓ mean fetal weight, ↓ crown–rump length.  
Mechanism: maternal and placental CYP-mediated ROS; possible partial inhibition of mitochondrial complex-I by β-carboline–like skeleton → energy deficit.  
Adversity: Adverse; growth retardation may partially recover post-natally but is linked to later life metabolic disease (IUGR).  
Monitorability: ultrasound biometry, birth weight.

3.3 Skeletal variations  
• Delayed ossification of sternebra(e), phalanges; wavy ribs. Observed with benzo­fused PAHs (e.g., benzo[a]pyrene) and isoquinolines; driven by fetal growth delay.  
Mechanism: secondary to growth retardation and Vitamin-D homeostasis disturbance via AhR activation.  
Adversity: Mostly adaptive (developmental delay) and reversible post-natal.  
Monitorability: prenatal ultrasound (limited), post-natal radiographs.

3.4 Structural malformations  
• Low-incidence cleft palate, limb flexures predicted (DEREK alert for “aryl-nitrogen heteroaromatic with PAH-like planarity”). Requires high peak exposure.  
Mechanism: AhR agonism → dysregulation of Wnt/FGF in craniofacial mesenchyme; excessive ROS during palate shelf elevation.  
Adversity: Adverse, irreversible; surgical correction possible.  
Monitorability: high-resolution ultrasound (wk 20 in human).

3.5 Functional / neuro-behavioral effects  
• Post-natal deficits in locomotor activity and learning (analogy: β-carboline alkaloids inhibit MAO-A/B and bind GABA-A sites).  
Mechanism: maternal/fetal MAO inhibition → elevated monoamines during neuro­development; oxidative deamination byproducts increase ROS; possible dopaminergic neuron sensitisation (rodent evidence for tetrahydroisoquinoline).  
Adversity: Adverse; partly reversible due to neuro-plasticity but often persistent.  
Monitorability: developmental neuro­behavioral testing (e.g., FOB, learning and memory assays).

4. Weight-of-evidence evaluation  

Hazard strength  
• Structural alerts: (+) for developmental toxicity (DEREK, confidence 0.63).  
• Read-across: several congeners show clear prenatal toxicity in rodents (LOAEL 25–75 mg kg-1 d-1).  
• Lack of negative data.  

Overall toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM to HIGH  
Overall confidence: MODERATE (data gap for test substance; reliance on analogue data and QSAR).

5. Reversibility & clinical monitoring summary  

Effect – Adverse/Adaptive – Reversible? – Clinical monitoring  
a) Embryo/fetal death – Adverse – Irreversible – Ultrasonography, hCG.  
b) Growth restriction – Adverse – Partially reversible – Ultrasound biometry, birth weight, paediatric follow-up.  
c) Skeletal delay – Adaptive – Reversible – Post-natal X-ray if needed.  
d) Cleft palate / limb malformations – Adverse – Irreversible (surgery) – Prenatal ultrasound, neonatal exam.  
e) Neuro-behavioral deficits – Adverse – Partially reversible – Developmental screening, neuro-psych testing.

6. Uncertainties & data gaps  
• No empirical kinetic data for the parent compound; placenta transfer inferred.  
• AhR affinity not experimentally confirmed; based on PAH analogy.  
• Interspecies differences in metabolism (CYP2D6, MAO) may modify risk in humans.  
• At the MTD maternal toxicity may confound developmental endpoints (cannot separate maternal-mediated vs direct fetal effect).

7. Conclusion  
At a daily MTD given orally throughout gestation, 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydro­benzo[f]isoquinoline is predicted to present a HIGH systemic availability and a MEDIUM–HIGH probability of causing adverse developmental outcomes, namely embryo-fetal death, growth retardation, delayed ossification, low-frequency structural malformations (cleft palate/limb defects) and post-natal neuro-behavioral changes. Mechanistically, bioactivation to reactive oxidative metabolites, AhR pathway perturbation and MAO inhibition constitute the most plausible drivers. Absent direct study data, this conclusion carries moderate confidence; targeted OECD TG 414 and kinetic studies would be required to refine the risk assessment.
