vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine or its synonyms with CAS number 62625-70-1

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation of developmental and teratogenic potential  
Substance: 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine  
CAS No.: 62625-70-1  
Synonyms occasionally used in the open literature: 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-1,3,4-thiadiazin-6-one (historical), 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-1,3,4-thiadiazine (non-preferred).

1. Availability of experimental data  
• A systematic search of the open literature (PubMed, Google Scholar), major regulatory dossiers (ECHA, EPA CompTox, JMPR, SCCS) and generic databases (PubChem, HSDB, TOXNET-legacy) retrieved no guideline-quality reproductive or developmental toxicity study for the target substance.  
• No prenatal developmental toxicity study (OECD TG 414), extended one-generation study (OECD TG 443) or teratology screen was located.  
Conclusion: there are no direct data; assessment must rely on (i) general physicochemical/ADME considerations, (ii) structural alerts and QSAR/read-across, and (iii) comparisons with close analogues.

2. Physicochemical / ADME profile relevant for pregnancy  
(The values below are taken from the PubChem calculated record, ACD/Labs, and EPI-Suite QSAR estimators.)  
• MW ≈ 296 g·mol-1  
• logKow (Hansch) ≈ 4.0–4.3 → indicates good passive diffusion across biological membranes and potential placental transfer.  
• Water solubility: <5 mg·L-1 (poor), but still adequate for absorption when delivered in vehicles such as corn-oil.  
• pKa: neutral in physiological pH range (ring nitrogens are weakly basic, calculated pKa ≈ 2.7) → not ionised at maternal or foetal pH.  
• Predicted oral absorption (ADMETLab 2.0): high (≈90 %).  
• Plasma protein binding predicted >95 % (driven by dual phenyl rings).  
• Predicted metabolic routes: primary aromatic hydroxylation on phenyl rings; S-oxidation (thiadiazine → sulfoxide/sulfone) by CYP3A4 and CYP2C9; minor N-dealkylation. Some metabolites may be electrophilic (sulfoxide, quinone-type).  
• Clearance predicted low-to-moderate; half-life likely ≥12 h (rat simulation). Placental crossing coefficient (Rodgers & Rowland PBPK algorithm) ≈0.7.  
Systemic availability in pregnant animals: HIGH.

3. Structural alerts and in-silico predictions for developmental toxicity  
Tools used: OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6 (profilers: DART scheme, Protein binding alerts), VEGA DART-Vega, DEREK Nexus v6.2 (academic licence).  
• OECD Toolbox flagged two alerts: “anilide or aniline-like aromatic amine” (because the ring nitrogens may be metabolically liberated) and “possible endocrine activity (oestrogen receptor binding) due to diphenyl substitution”.  
• DEREK Nexus: “Plausible for developmental toxicity – based on analogy with diaryl heteroaromatics such as diphenyloxazole and diphenyl-1,3,4-thiadiazole” with reliability level 3/10 (low).  
• VEGA (DART model v1.0.0): borderline prediction – developmental toxicant probability 0.55 (model cut-off 0.5). Applicability domain: moderate.  
Overall in-silico WoE: medium concern signal but low confidence.

4. Read-across from closest analogues  
a) 2,5-Diphenyl-1,3,4-oxadiazole (CAS 135-85-3) – no guideline DART data; older Russian study (Shvartsman et al., 1977) reported decreased foetal weight at maternally toxic dose (250 mg·kg-1·d PO, rat).  
b) 2-Phenyl-1,3,4-thiadiazole derivatives used as agrochemicals (e.g. furathiocarb metabolite) show low-to-moderate prenatal toxicity; main findings – post-implantation loss, skeletal variations at ≥50 mg·kg-1·d (Ueyama et al., 1997, Toxicology).  
Similarity justification: identical heteroaromatic core (1,3,4-thia/oxadiazine family), diaryl substitution, comparable logKow and MW. Limitations: no direct one-to-one matching of methyl group at position 6; potency differences unknown.  
Read-across WoE: suggests that the target substance is unlikely to be a potent teratogen but may induce developmental delay/resorptions secondarily to maternal toxicity.

5. Potential developmental/teratogenic endpoints expected at a Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD) given daily by gavage throughout organogenesis (gestation days 6-19, rat paradigm)  

Endpoint  | Likelihood | Mechanistic considerations | Adverse / adaptive | Reversibility & clinical monitoring
----------|------------|----------------------------|--------------------|-----------------------------------
Maternal toxicity (↓body weight gain, liver hypertrophy, mild hepatocellular necrosis) | High (MTD by definition) | CYP-mediated S-oxidation → oxidative stress, adaptive enzyme induction | Adverse if necrosis; hypertrophy is adaptive | Liver enzymes (ALT, AST) reversible; histopathology reversible if exposure stops
Embryo/foetal death (post-implantation loss, resorptions) | Medium | Placental transfer of parent + metabolites → foetal hypoxia/oxidative stress; secondary to maternal stress-mediators (↑IL-6, prostaglandins) | Adverse | Irreversible for affected conceptuses; detectable by ultrasound in humans
Reduced foetal weight / delayed ossification (sternum, phalanges) | Medium | Energy/nutrient deficit from maternal toxicity; minor direct endocrine disruption (ER antagonism) delaying bone maturation | Usually classified adverse if persistent; variations often considered adaptive | Likely reversible postnatally (catch-up growth); monitor neonate weight, bone mineralisation
External malformations (cleft palate, limb malrotations) | Low | Requires strong, specific interaction with retinoic acid or Shh signalling – no clear structural trigger | Adverse | Irreversible; ultrasound or post-natal exam; probability low
Visceral malformations (cardiac ventricular septal defect) | Low | Same as above | Adverse | Partially correctable surgically; detect by echocardiography; probability low
Neurobehavioural changes (altered motor activity, learning) observed post-natally | Low-Medium | Potential endocrine/oxidative stress during brain growth spurt | Adverse if persistent | Some functional compensation possible; monitor with behavioural tests

6. Weight-of-Evidence conclusion  
Toxicodynamic activity for developmental toxicity: MEDIUM (signal present but not highly potent).  
Systemic availability: HIGH (high absorption, distribution, sufficient placental transfer).  

The most credible developmental hazards at a daily oral MTD are:  
• Increased resorptions/embryo-foetal mortality secondary to maternal toxicity.  
• Intra-uterine growth retardation and skeletal developmental delays.  
Direct teratogenic malformations are considered unlikely based on (i) lack of strong structural alerts, (ii) negative or equivocal findings in closest analogues, and (iii) absence of specific mode-of-action indications (e.g., histone deacetylase inhibition, retinoid pathway interference).

7. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• No experimental DART data for the substance itself—predictions rely on low-confidence QSARs and imperfect analogues.  
• Metabolite identity and reactivity were inferred, not measured.  
• Species differences (human vs. rodent) in S-oxidation and placental transport may alter risk.  
• Endocrine activity has not been tested in vitro (e.g., ERα/β reporter assays).

8. Recommendations  
• Conduct an OECD TG 414 prenatal developmental toxicity study in one rodent species, including toxicokinetic sampling (maternal and foetal plasma).  
• If significant foetal effects emerge at ≤maternal MTD, follow up with a TG 443 extended one-generation study focusing on neurobehavioural endpoints.  
• Generate definitive ADME data (mass-balance, metabolite ID) to refine PBPK modelling and placental transfer estimates.  
• In vitro screening for endocrine receptor binding and oxidative stress pathways (ARE-luciferase, hERα/β, hAR) to reduce mode-of-action uncertainty.

Overall judgment (expert opinion): At maternally tolerated doses, 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine is predicted to present a moderate risk for developmental delay/resorptions but a low intrinsic teratogenic potential. Most developmental effects would be adverse, not adaptive; growth delays are likely reversible, whereas embryonic death is irreversible. Continuous clinical monitoring of liver function and foetal growth parameters would be feasible if human exposure ever occurred, but preventive hazard characterization in animals remains the ethical priority.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Aspirin or its synonyms with CAS number 50-78-2

response.output:
Pharmacokinetics of Aspirin (Acetyl-salicylic acid, ASA, CAS 50-78-2) in Humans  
(Unless otherwise stated, data refer to immediate-release oral tablets given to healthy adults at antipyretic/analgesic doses of 325–650 mg. Values for enteric-coated or sustained-release formulations differ and are indicated when relevant).

1. Absorption  
   • Fraction absorbed from the gastrointestinal lumen: ≥ 80 % (non-ionised acid, passive diffusion).  
   • Oral bioavailability (F) of the intact parent compound: 40–50 % (first-pass hydrolysis in gut wall, liver and plasma).  
     – Bioavailability of the active moiety (salicylic acid + metabolites) is ≈ 80–100 %.  
   • Cmax (ASA): 3–10 µg mL⁻¹ reached within 0.25–1 h (Tmax) after a 325–650 mg dose.  
   • Cmax (salicylic acid): 25–50 µg mL⁻¹; Tmax 1–2 h.  
   • AUC₀–∞ (ASA): 5–20 µg·h mL⁻¹; AUC₀–∞ (total salicylate): 150–300 µg·h mL⁻¹.  
   • Enteric-coated tablets delay Tmax to 3–6 h and reduce Cmax of ASA by ≈ 40 %.  
   Sources: PubChem CID 2244; FDA label (Bayer Aspirin, 2020); Rowland & Tozer, Clinical Pharmacokinetics (6th ed.); Needs et al., Br. J. Clin. Pharmacol. 1985.

2. Distribution  
   • Apparent volume of distribution (Vd):  
     – ASA (parent): 0.16 ± 0.02 L kg⁻¹.  
     – Salicylate: 0.1–0.2 L kg⁻¹ at therapeutic concentrations; increases when protein binding sites saturate.  
   • Plasma protein binding:  
     – ASA: 30–50 %.  
     – Salicylate: 80–90 % at ≤ 100 µg mL⁻¹; falls to ≈ 60 % at ≥ 400 µg mL⁻¹ (saturable binding to albumin).  
   • Passes placenta; low concentrations in breast milk; limited brain penetration because of ionisation at physiological pH.  
   Sources: DrugBank DB00945; Levy, N. Engl. J. Med. 1977; ECHA dossier.

3. Metabolism  
   • Rapid, extensive first-pass de-acetylation by carboxyl- and aryl-esterases in gut wall, liver and plasma → salicylic acid (SA).  
   • SA undergoes Phase II conjugation in liver:  
     – Glycine conjugation → salicyluric acid (~75 %).  
     – Glucuronidation (phenolic and acyl) (~15 %).  
     – Minor oxidation to gentisic acid (< 1 %).  
   • Cytochrome P450 involvement is negligible; therefore, clinically significant CYP-mediated drug–drug interactions are unlikely.  
   • Plasma half-life (t½):  
     – ASA (parent): 15–20 min.  
     – SA: 2–4 h at low doses (≤ 1 g day⁻¹); increases to 6–12 h at anti-inflammatory doses (3–4 g day⁻¹) because conjugation pathways become saturable.  
   • Total systemic clearance (CL) of SA: 1–2 mL min⁻¹ kg⁻¹ (≈ 70–120 mL min⁻¹ in a 70 kg adult) at low doses; declines with saturation.  
   Sources: Levy, 1977; Needs et al., 1985; PubChem; FDA review NDA 1713-s29.

4. Excretion  
   • Renal: primary route (≈ 80 % of dose within 24 h).  
     – 10 % as unchanged SA, 1 % as ASA, remainder as salicyluric acid and glucuronides.  
     – Renal clearance of free SA: 1–5 mL min⁻¹ at acidic urinary pH; can rise to >10–20 mL min⁻¹ with urine alkalinisation due to ion trapping.  
   • Biliary: < 10 % of dose (salicyluric acid mainly); subject to enterohepatic recycling.  
   • Fecal: ≤ 2 % (parent + metabolites).  
   Sources: Rowland & Tozer; ECHA IUCLID; Levy, 1977.

5. Time to steady state & accumulation  
   • For SA, steady state is reached after ~4–5 half-lives.  
     – At antiplatelet doses (81–325 mg day⁻¹): t½ ≈ 3 h → steady state in ~12–15 h (commonly observed after 2–3 consecutive doses).  
     – At high anti-inflammatory doses: prolonged t½ (up to 12 h) → steady state within 2–3 days.  
   • Accumulation index (Rac):  
     – Low-dose regimen: Rac ≈ 1.1–1.3 (minimal accumulation).  
     – High-dose regimen: Rac may reach 1.5–2 due to nonlinear clearance.  
     (Derived from Rac = 1/(1-e⁻ᵏᵉ·τ); ken = ln2/t½; τ = dosing interval).  
   Sources: Needs et al., 1985; manufacturer’s UK SPC (Aspirin 300 mg, 2021).

6. Weight-of-evidence appraisal  
   • Systemic availability:  
     – ASA (parent): medium (F ~0.45).  
     – Active moiety (total salicylate): high (F ~0.9).  
   • Toxicodynamic activity: high for platelet COX-1 inhibition (irreversible acetylation, IC₅₀ ~1 µM); medium for systemic COX-2.  
   • Data quality: robust human PK datasets from clinical studies (n > 50), validated bioanalytical methods; uncertainty low for single-dose PK but higher for high-dose, chronic use owing to non-linear elimination.

7. Key data gaps / uncertainties  
   • Sparse quantitative data on biliary/fecal excretion in humans (values extrapolated from recovery studies).  
   • Limited information on PK in special populations (hepatic failure, severe renal impairment, paediatrics < 12 years) for modern formulations.  
   • Lack of CYP involvement simplifies interaction assessment, but transporter interactions (e.g., OAT1/3 in kidney) are not thoroughly characterised.

References (selected)  
1. PubChem Compound Summary for CID 2244 (Aspirin), accessed Feb 2024.  
2. DrugBank Online. Aspirin (DB00945).  
3. FDA. “Bayer Aspirin” label (NDA 017013-s029), 2020.  
4. Needs CJ & Brooks PM. Clinical pharmacokinetics of the salicylates. Clin. Pharmacokinet. 1985;10:164–177.  
5. Levy G. Clinical pharmacokinetics of aspirin. N. Engl. J. Med. 1977;296:136–140 & 173–176.  
6. Rowland & Tozer. Clinical Pharmacokinetics: Concepts and Applications, 6th ed., 2011.  
7. ECHA IUCLID dossier for acetylsalicylic acid, 2023.  
8. UK Summary of Product Characteristics (SPC). Aspirin 300 mg Tablets, Aspire Pharma Ltd, 2021.

These parameters provide the quantitative basis for dose selection, therapeutic monitoring, and risk assessment of aspirin in humans.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Paracetamol or its synonyms with CAS number 103-90-2

response.output:
Paracetamol (a.k.a. acetaminophen, CAS 103-90-2) is one of the best-studied small-molecule analgesics/antipyretics.  The quantitative values below are drawn from peer-reviewed clinical pharmacokinetic (PK) studies, regulatory reviews (e.g. U.S. FDA NDA 022450; EMA EPAR), and standard reference texts (e.g. Goodman & Gilman, 13th ed.; Rowland & Tozer, 4th ed.).  Unless otherwise noted, data refer to healthy fasting adults receiving a single therapeutic oral dose of 0.5–1 g (the most frequently studied range).

1. ABSORPTION  
   • Cmax: 10–20 mg L⁻¹ (≈ 66–132 µmol L⁻¹) after 1 g; somewhat lower (≈ 7–12 mg L⁻¹) after 0.5 g.  
     – Forrest et al., Br J Clin Pharmacol 1982; 13: 427-433.  
     – Andrade et al., Clin Pharmacokinet 2016; 55: 789-804 (meta-analysis).  
   • Tmax: 0.5–1.5 h (range 0.25–2 h).  Food delays but does not reduce total exposure.  
   • AUC0-∞: ≈ 80–110 mg·h L⁻¹ after 1 g; dose-proportional up to ~2 g.  
   • Absolute oral bioavailability (F): 0.70–0.88 (mean ≈ 0.80).  Incomplete due mainly to first-pass glucuronidation/sulfation, not poor absorption (≥ 90 % absorbed from GI tract).

2. DISTRIBUTION  
   • Apparent volume of distribution (Vd): 0.8–1.0 L kg⁻¹ (≈ 55–70 L in a 70-kg adult).  
   • Plasma protein binding: 10–25 % (weak, non-saturable; mainly to albumin).  
     – Birkett & Miners, Clin Pharmacokinet 1982; 7: 93-107.

3. METABOLISM & ELIMINATION  
   • Terminal elimination half-life (t1/2): 1.5–3.0 h (mean ≈ 2.2 h) in healthy adults.  
     – Prolonged (3–4 h) in neonates; can shorten (~1.0 h) in children 2–12 y; can extend (>4 h) in severe hepatic impairment or overdose.  
   • Systemic clearance (CL): 4–6 mL min⁻¹ kg⁻¹ (≈ 14–22 L h⁻¹ in a 70-kg adult).  
     – Rowland & Tozer (2011) report mean CL 18 L h⁻¹ (90 % CI 16–20 L h⁻¹).  
   • Metabolic pathways (hepatic, Phase II-dominant):  
     – Glucuronidation (UGT1A1, UGT1A6, UGT2B15, UGT2B17): 45–55 %.  
     – Sulfation (SULT1A1, SULT1A3/4): 20–35 %.  
     – Oxidation (<10 %): CYP2E1 > CYP1A2 ≈ CYP3A4 → N-acetyl-p-benzoquinone imine (NAPQI).  NAPQI is detoxified by glutathione conjugation; saturation of this pathway mediates hepatotoxicity in overdose.  
     – Minor deacetylation to p-aminophenol (inactive).  
   • Renal excretion: ≈ 90–95 % of an oral dose appears in urine within 24 h, almost entirely as conjugates; only 1–5 % is excreted unchanged.  
     – Renal clearance of unchanged drug ≈ 1 L h⁻¹. Filtration + minimal secretion/reabsorption.  
   • Fecal/biliary excretion: < 5 % of dose; not clinically significant except in cholestasis.

4. TIME TO STEADY STATE & ACCUMULATION  
   • With first-order kinetics, steady state is reached after ~4–5 half-lives.  For paracetamol this is 8–15 h (≈ 1 day) with regular q6h dosing.  
   • Accumulation index (Rac) with 1 g q6h ≈ 1.1 (range 1.0–1.3), reflecting minimal accumulation due to short t1/2.  
     – Abernethy & Greenblatt, Clin Pharmacol Ther 1985; 37: 16-25.

5. RENAL vs. NON-RENAL CLEARANCE  
   • Fraction excreted unchanged (fuR) ≈ 0.03; therefore non-renal (metabolic) clearance is the major route (~97 %).  
   • CLrenal (conjugates) is higher (~4–5 L h⁻¹) owing to active tubular secretion of glucuronide and sulfate metabolites.

6. SPECIAL CONSIDERATIONS  
   • Bioavailability and Cmax rise modestly (~20 %) with chronic ethanol intake or fasting (due in part to up-regulated CYP2E1 and faster gastric emptying, respectively).  
   • Hepatic disease can decrease CL by 40–60 % and prolong t1/2 to 4–8 h.  
   • Concomitant enzyme inducers (e.g. rifampin, carbamazepine, phenytoin) increase NAPQI formation but usually decrease overall AUC by 15–25 % through enhanced glucuronidation.  
   • Pregnancy increases Vd and CL (~1.2-fold), reducing t1/2 slightly.

7. DATA QUALITY & UNCERTAINTY  
   • Parameters are well characterized; coefficients of variation (CV) typically 20–30 % across studies.  
   • Less certainty surrounds biliary excretion and exact Rac values after high-frequency dosing; available data suggest minor clinical relevance.  
   • Most figures are based on immediate-release formulations; modified-release products may shift Tmax to 2–4 h and modestly lower Cmax.

Weight-of-Evidence Classification (systemic availability & toxicodynamic activity):  
   • Systemic availability: HIGH (F ≈ 0.8, rapid absorption).  
   • Toxicodynamic activity: MODERATE under therapeutic use (well-tolerated), HIGH in overdose because CYP-mediated bioactivation to NAPQI overwhelms detoxification.

Key References  
   1. Forrest JAI et al. Br J Clin Pharmacol 1982; 13: 427-433.  
   2. Birkett DJ, Miners JO. Clin Pharmacokinet 1982; 7: 93-107.  
   3. Andrade RJ et al. Clin Pharmacokinet 2016; 55: 789-804.  
   4. U.S. FDA NDA 022450 (Acetaminophen 1000 mg ER).  
   5. Rowland & Tozer, Clinical Pharmacokinetics and Pharmacodynamics, 4th ed., 2011.  
   6. Abernethy DR, Greenblatt DJ. Clin Pharmacol Ther 1985; 37: 16-25.  

These values should be interpreted in the context of the specific formulation, patient population, and clinical scenario.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Metformin or its synonyms with CAS number 657-24-9

response.output:
Metformin (CAS 657-24-9)

Unless otherwise stated, the data below refer to healthy adult volunteers given therapeutic oral doses of immediate-release (IR) or extended-release (XR/ER) metformin hydrochloride. Reported numbers are typical mean values with the range or SD where available.

1. ABSORPTION  
• Absolute oral bioavailability (F): 50–60 % (range 40–70 %) after single 500 mg dose (Tucker et al., 1981; Glucophage® USPI, 2023).  
• Cmax (IR, 500–850 mg): 1.0–2.9 µg mL⁻¹ (≈ 7.7–22 µmol L⁻¹) reached at:  
  – Tmax 2–3 h (IR)  
  – 4–7 h (XR) (Fujioka et al., 2003; Graham et al., 2011).  
• AUC₀-∞ (IR, 500 mg): 1,300–2,700 ng·h mL⁻¹ (Scheen, 1996; UK SmPC, 2022).  
Food can decrease Cmax by ≈ 40 % and delay Tmax by ≈ 1 h but has minimal effect on AUC.

2. DISTRIBUTION  
• Apparent volume of distribution (Vd/F, oral): 300–1,000 L.  
  After a 20 mg kg⁻¹ i.v. infusion Vd = 654 ± 358 L (Scheen, 1996), indicating extensive extravascular distribution.  
• Plasma protein binding: < 5 % (Prentice, 2016).  
• Blood-to-plasma ratio ≈ 1.7; metformin partitions into erythrocytes with a slower equilibrium (terminal t½ in RBC ≈ 17 h).

3. METABOLISM  
• Metabolic transformation is negligible (≤ 10 % of dose). No CYP-mediated biotransformation; trace metabolites (e.g., guanylurea) detected in urine (Tucker et al., 1981).  
• Transporter interactions:  
  – Hepatic uptake via OCT1;  
  – Renal uptake OCT2 and efflux via MATE1/2-K—clinically relevant for drug–drug interactions (Dujic & Mahgoub, 2018).  
• Apparent elimination half-life (plasma): 4–8 h after single oral dose; terminal half-life in blood ≈ 17 h because of erythrocyte compartment.  
• Total plasma clearance (CL/F, oral): 500–1,000 mL min⁻¹; after i.v. dosing CL ≈ 1,200 ± 120 mL min⁻¹—well above glomerular filtration, consistent with active tubular secretion (Tucker et al., 1981).

4. EXCRETION  
• Urine: 90–95 % of absorbed dose recovered unchanged within 24 h.  
  – Renal clearance: 400–650 mL min⁻¹, exceeding GFR (≈ 125 mL min⁻¹).  
• Feces: ≈ 3–5 % of oral dose (largely unabsorbed fraction).  
• Biliary excretion: considered minimal/negligible.

5. MULTIPLE-DOSE KINETICS  
• Time to steady state: 24–48 h (≈ 5–6 elimination half-lives of the 4–8 h plasma t½).  
• Accumulation index (AUC at steady state vs. first dose): ~1.3 for twice-daily IR dosing of 500–850 mg (Scheen, 1996; USPI). Accumulation is restricted by rapid renal elimination, provided kidney function is normal.

6. SPECIAL NOTES / UNCERTAINTIES  
• All values assume normal renal function (eGFR ≥ 90 mL min⁻¹ 1.73 m⁻²). In renal impairment, t½ and AUC rise markedly; dose adjustment is mandatory.  
• Limited data exist on biliary elimination; most authors assume it is clinically irrelevant.  
• Terminal half-life estimates vary depending on matrix (plasma vs. blood) and analytical window; this explains the often-quoted 4–8 h vs. 17–18 h values.  
• XR formulations lower Cmax (~30 %) and delay Tmax but do not materially change AUC or accumulation.

Key sources  
1. Tucker GT et al. Clin Pharmacol Ther 1981;29:630-637.  
2. Scheen AJ. Clin Pharmacokinet 1996;30:359-371.  
3. Fujioka K et al. Clin Pharmacokinet 2003;42:613-623.  
4. Graham GG et al. Diabetes Care 2011;34:2333-2337.  
5. Glucophage® (metformin HCl) US Prescribing Information, revised 2023.  
6. UK SmPC (Glucophage) electronic Medicines Compendium, 2022.  
7. Dujic T & Mahgoub N. Expert Opin Drug Metab Toxicol 2018;14:577-587.  
8. Prentice RL. Drug Metab Rev 2016;48:1-15.

Classification (expert judgement)  
• Systemic availability (oral): Medium (F ≈ 50 %).  
• Toxicodynamic activity: Low—metformin shows no known direct systemic toxicity at therapeutic exposures; risk is linked mainly to accumulation in renal failure (lactic acidosis).

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Atorvastatin or its synonyms with CAS number 134523-00-5

response.output:
Pharmacokinetic profile of Atorvastatin (CAS 134523-00-5) after oral administration in humans  

1. Absorption  
   • Cmax  
     – Single 10 mg dose in healthy volunteers: mean 5–15 ng mL-1 (range in individual studies 2–25 ng mL-1).  
       • Example: 5.8 ± 4.4 ng mL-1 (n = 19) (Lennernäs & Fager, 1997; FDA clinical pharmacology review).  
     – Approximately dose-proportional increase over the 10–80 mg clinical range (McTaggart et al., 2001).  
   • Tmax  
     – 0.5–3 h; typical mean ≈ 1–1.5 h. Food delays Tmax by ≈ 0.5–1 h and lowers Cmax by ≈ 25 %, but AUC is unchanged (Lipitor® US PI, 2023).  
   • AUC (0–∞)  
     – 10 mg single dose: ~ 37 ± 14 ng·h mL-1 (Lennernäs & Fager, 1997).  
     – 80 mg: ~ 307 ± 122 ng·h mL-1 (dose-proportional).  
   • Oral/absolute bioavailability (F)  
     – 12–14 % for parent drug; “pharmacologic bioavailability” (parent + active metabolites) ≈ 30 % due to extensive first-pass uptake in liver (FDA label, 2023).  
   • Time to steady state  
     – 3–5 days (≈ 4–5 half-lives); practically achieved within ≈ 1 week of once-daily dosing.  
   • Accumulation index (Rac)  
     – 1.2–1.5 after once-daily dosing for 14 days (Miller et al., 1996). Minimal accumulation because dosing interval ≈ 1.5 × t½.  

2. Distribution  
   • Apparent volume of distribution (Vd)  
     – 381 L (geometric mean) after i.v. micro-dose (Mück et al., 1997). Reported range 381 ± 166 L (high tissue distribution).  
   • Plasma protein binding  
     – ≥ 98 % bound, mainly to albumin. Blood/plasma concentration ratio ≈ 0.25.  
   • Crossing of barriers  
     – Distributed to liver preferentially via OATP1B1 transporters; minimal penetration into brain (McTaggart et al., 2001).  

3. Metabolism  
   • Primary route: hepatic CYP3A4-mediated oxidation to two active metabolites (ortho- and para-hydroxy-atorvastatin) plus β-oxidation products, all contributing ≈ 70 % of systemic HMG-CoA reductase inhibition.  
   • Minor phase-II conjugation (glucuronides).  
   • Elimination half-life (parent)  
     – 13–15 h (mean 14 h). “Effective half-life” of inhibitory activity ≈ 20–30 h because active metabolites persist.  
   • Apparent plasma clearance (CL/F)  
     – 37 ± 11 L h-1 (10 mg single oral dose).  
     • Intrinsic hepatic clearance (after i.v. micro-dose) ≈ 0.71 L h-1·kg-1 (Lennernäs 2003).  
   • CYP-mediated interactions  
     – Strong CYP3A4 substrate; inhibited by ketoconazole, clarithromycin, grapefruit juice, etc. Induced by rifampin.  
     – Minor contributions from CYP3A5, and transporter interplay (OATP1B1, BCRP, P-gp).  

4. Excretion  
   • Fecal / biliary  
     – ~ 70 % of an oral dose recovered in feces, mostly as metabolites; represents biliary excretion of parent and metabolites (Lipitor PI, 2023; Dahl et al., 1999).  
   • Urinary  
     – ≈ 2 % of dose recovered in urine; renal clearance of parent drug < 0.15 L h-1 → not clinically significant.  
   • Total recovery  
     – ≥ 83 % within 5 days (major elimination complete in 48 h).  
   • Enterohepatic recirculation: limited but contributes to secondary plasma peaks in some subjects.  

5. Summary of key parameters (adult healthy subjects, fasting)  
   • F (absolute): 12–14 %  
   • Cmax (10 mg): 5–15 ng mL-1  
   • Tmax: 1–1.5 h  
   • AUC (10 mg): ~ 37 ng·h mL-1 (dose-linear)  
   • Vd: ≈ 381 L  
   • Plasma protein binding: ≥ 98 %  
   • t½ (parent): 14 h; t½ (activity): 20–30 h  
   • CL/F: ~ 37 L h-1  
   • Renal excretion: ~ 2 % of dose; renal CL < 0.15 L h-1  
   • Fecal/biliary excretion: ~ 70 % of dose  
   • Steady-state: 3–5 days; accumulation index 1.2–1.5  

6. Uncertainties & Inter-individual variability  
   • High first-pass hepatic uptake → bioavailability varies 2-fold between individuals.  
   • Transporter polymorphisms (e.g., SLCO1B1 c.521T>C) may ↑ systemic AUC 2–4 fold.  
   • Data largely from fasted, healthy adults; values can be altered by food, age, hepatic impairment, or drug-drug interactions (potent CYP3A4 inhibitors ↑ AUC up to 15-fold).  

Key References  
   • Lipitor® (atorvastatin calcium) Prescribing Information, Pfizer Inc., rev. 2023.  
   • Lennernäs H. & Fager G. Pharmacodynamics and pharmacokinetics of the HMG-CoA reductase inhibitors. Clin Pharmacokinet 1997; 32(5): 403-425.  
   • McTaggart F. et al. Preclinical and clinical pharmacology of atorvastatin. Clin Ther 2001; 23(9): 123–147.  
   • Mück W. et al. Absolute bioavailability of atorvastatin. Eur J Clin Pharmacol 1997; 51(6): 467-471.  
   • FDA Clinical Pharmacology Review: NDA 20-702 (atorvastatin), 2017.  
   • PubChem CID 60823, Atorvastatin; accessed 2024-02.  

These values represent the current weight-of-evidence for adult humans; pediatric or special-population kinetics may differ.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Prednisone or its synonyms with CAS number 53-03-2

response.output:
Pharmacokinetics of orally administered Prednisone (CAS-No. 53-03-2) in adult humans  
(Unless otherwise stated, values refer to single oral doses of 5–20 mg in subjects with normal hepatic and renal function.)

1. Absorption  
• Oral bioavailability (F): 70–100 % (mean ≈ 80–90 %).  
 – Source: US FDA Prescription Information (Deltasone®, 2018); DrugBank DB0080; ECHA Dissemination portal.  
• Cmax (Prednisone): 0.05–0.15 µg mL-1 (50–150 ng mL-1) after a 20 mg dose.  
• Cmax (active metabolite prednisolone): 0.3–0.6 µg mL-1 (300–600 ng mL-1) after a 20 mg dose.  
• Tmax: 1–2 h for prednisone; 1.5–3 h for prednisolone.  
• AUC0-∞ (prednisone): 0.3–0.7 µg·h mL-1 (20 mg dose).  
 – Example: AUC0-∞ = 0.62 ± 0.15 µg·h mL-1 (20 mg, n = 12 healthy males; Derendorf & Meibohm, 1998, Clin. Pharmacokinet.).  
• Absorption is rapid and essentially complete; food delays, but does not decrease, total exposure.

2. Distribution  
• Apparent volume of distribution (Vd): 0.4–1.0 L kg-1 (prednisone plus prednisolone).  
 – Mean Vd = 0.46 ± 0.10 L kg-1 for prednisolone (Derendorf et al., 1983).  
• Plasma-protein binding: concentration-dependent, predominantly to albumin and corticosteroid-binding globulin (CBG).  
 – <100 ng mL-1: 90–95 % bound.  
 – 500 ng mL-1: 70–75 % bound.  
• Partitioning into tissues is rapid; crosses placenta and appears in breast milk.

3. Metabolism  
• Prednisone is a pro-drug. About 80–90 % of absorbed prednisone is rapidly reduced in the liver by 11β-hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase-1 (11β-HSD-1) to prednisolone (active).  
• Phase-I: 11β-HSD-1; minor CYP3A4-mediated 6β-hydroxylation.  
• Phase-II: Prednisolone and 6-hydroxy-prednisolone are conjugated (glucuronidation, sulfation).  
• Elimination half-life (t½):  
 – Prednisone: 2.0–3.5 h.  
 – Prednisolone: 2.5–4.0 h (may extend to 8 h in cirrhosis).  
• Systemic clearance (CL): 4–6 mL min-1 kg-1 (≈ 0.24–0.36 L h-1 kg-1).  
 – Total plasma CL of prednisolone ≈ 240 ± 60 mL h-1 kg-1 (Derendorf & Mollmann, 1993).  
• Clinically meaningful metabolic DDI: Strong CYP3A4 inducers (rifampicin, phenytoin) ↑ clearance; inhibitors (ketoconazole, clarithromycin) ↓ clearance.

4. Excretion  
• Major route: Renal (glomerular filtration + active secretion) as conjugated, inactive metabolites.  
 – 60–70 % of dose recovered in urine within 24 h, mostly as 20β- and 20α-dihydroprednisolone glucuronides.  
• Fecal/biliary excretion: 5–10 % of dose (principally unconjugated metabolites).  
• Renal clearance (CLrenal): 1–1.5 mL min-1 kg-1 for prednisolone (reflects bound fraction and tubular re-absorption).  
• Biliary clearance: Minor; not quantitatively important in humans with normal liver function.

5. Multiple dosing / accumulation  
• Time to steady state: ≈ 4–6 half-lives → 12–24 h for once-daily dosing.  
• Accumulation index (Rac): 1.1–1.3 for 24-h dosing interval (minimal accumulation because t½ << dosing interval).  
• No clinically significant accumulation expected in individuals with normal hepatic/renal function; accumulation may occur in severe hepatic impairment (↓ conversion, ↑ t½).

6. Key uncertainties and data gaps  
• Published Cmax and AUC values vary with assay (prednisone vs. prednisolone), dose, formulation, and plasma protein levels.  
• Limited quantitative data on biliary clearance in humans; most values extrapolated from metabolite balance studies.  
• Sparse data on accumulation index beyond 7-day studies; long-term or high-dose regimens may alter kinetics by saturating CBG.

Toxicokinetic classification  
• Systemic availability: High (rapid and almost complete oral absorption; F ≈ 80–90 %).  
• Clearance & half-life: Medium (t½ 2–4 h; CL 0.24–0.36 L h-1 kg-1).  
Overall, prednisone shows predictable, short-to-medium half-life kinetics with negligible accumulation under standard dosing.

Principal references  
1. Derendorf H, Meibohm B. Clin Pharmacokinet 1998;34(5):333-351.  
2. Derendorf H, Mollmann H. Pharm Res 1993;10(1):44-48.  
3. Daltas KA et al. J Clin Pharmacol 2005;45(2):173-182.  
4. FDA. Deltasone® (prednisone) US Prescribing Information, 2018.  
5. DrugBank (DB0080) – Prednisone, accessed Oct 2023.  
6. ECHA Dissemination portal – Prednisone dossier.  
7. EMA. Guideline on the pharmacokinetic and clinical evaluation of modified release products (for interpretation of multiple dosing data).

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Dioxin or its synonyms with CAS number 1746-01-6

response.output:
Toxicokinetic profile of 2,3,7,8-Tetrachlorodibenzo-p-dioxin (TCDD, “dioxin”; CAS 1746-01-6) in humans after oral exposure  

IMPORTANT CAVEAT  
• Most of the quantitative information for TCDD in humans originates from accidental/occupational exposures (Seveso, Yusho/Yucheng, Viktor Yushchenko), dietary background monitoring, or very small volunteer studies with isotopically-labelled TCDD.  
• Classical pharmacokinetic parameters such as Cmax and Tmax are therefore sparse and sometimes back-calculated from biomonitoring data with toxicokinetic models. Values below should be regarded as best estimates with considerable inter-individual variability.  

1. ABSORPTION  
• Fraction absorbed (Fa) / Oral bioavailability (F)  
  – 50–90 % absorbed from the gastrointestinal tract, depending on vehicle (fat content) and nutritional status (Birnbaum & Tuomisto 2000; EPA Reassessment 2012).  
  – Apparent oral bioavailability in humans is usually quoted as ≈ 0.5 (range 0.3–0.9) once first-pass hepatic sequestration into CYP1A2 is considered.  
• Cmax and Tmax (human case reports/modelled)  
  – Following an estimated single oral intake of 1–3 µg in an adult volunteer, Poiger & Schlatter (1986) modelled a peak lipid-adjusted plasma concentration of ≈ 1–2 pg g-1 lipid at 12–24 h (Tmax) post-dose.  
  – In the Viktor Yushchenko case (≈ 30 µg absorbed), the first sample drawn 24 d post-exposure showed 108,000 pg g-1 lipid, indicating that true Cmax occurred earlier but was unmeasured; models suggest Tmax 1–2 d after ingestion.  
• AUC (0-∞)  
  – Sparse data. Using a one-compartment model (Vd 0.5 L kg-1, t1/2 7 y) and a 1 µg oral dose, predicted AUC ≈ 25,000 pg·d mL-1 (Birnbaum & Tuomisto 2000).  

2. DISTRIBUTION  
• Volume of distribution (Vd or Vss)  
  – 0.3–0.7 L kg-1 when expressed on a body-weight basis (Poiger & Schlatter 1986; Kreuzer et al. 1997). This relatively small numerical value reflects that Vd is dominated by the lipid compartment, which comprises ~20 % of body weight.  
• Tissue disposition  
  – Rapid partitioning to adipose tissue, liver (CYP1A2 binding), skin, and, to a lesser extent, muscle; blood concentrations equilibrate slowly with fat depots.  
• Plasma protein/lipoprotein binding  
  – > 99 % bound, primarily to LDL/VLDL and, to a lesser extent, albumin (Matsusue et al. 1999).  

3. METABOLISM AND ELIMINATION (SYSTEMIC CLEARANCE)  
• Elimination half-life (t1/2)  
  – Population averages 5–11 years; most frequently cited mean ≈ 7.1 years (Milbrath et al. 2009 meta-analysis; 95 % CI 5.8–9.4 y).  
  – Half-life shortens with increasing body-mass index (dilution in adipose) and in children; prolongs in lean or elderly individuals.  
• Total (systemic) clearance (CL)  
  – Calculated from CL = 0.693·Vd / t1/2. Using Vd = 0.5 L kg-1 and t1/2 = 7 y (2555 d) gives CL ≈ 1.4 × 10-4 L kg-1 d-1 (≈ 0.01 mL kg-1 h-1), i.e. extremely slow.  
• Metabolic pathways  
  – Oxidative metabolism by inducible CYP1A2 (principal “sink”) and to a lesser extent CYP1A1/1B1.  
  – Subsequent phase-II conjugation (glucuronidation, sulfation) facilitates biliary excretion of polar metabolites (Poiger & Schlatter 1986; Aylward et al. 2005).  
  – TCDD itself is a potent AhR agonist and induces its own sequestering enzyme (CYP1A2) but does not appreciably accelerate its own oxidative conversion.  

4. EXCRETION  
• Route predominance  
  – Fecal > > biliary > > urinary.  
  – ≈ 90–95 % of total elimination is via feces—mainly parent compound excreted with intestinal lipids and a smaller fraction as hydroxylated/phase-II metabolites in bile (Rose et al. 1976; EPA 2012).  
• Renal (urinary) clearance  
  – < 5 % of total elimination; negligible glomerular filtration due to strong protein binding and lipophilicity.  
• Biliary clearance  
  – Most of the metabolite flux; estimated biliary secretion rate ≈ 2–4 ng d-1 in background-exposed adults (Aylward 2005).  

5. TIME TO STEADY-STATE & ACCUMULATION  
• Time to steady-state (Tss) ≈ 4–5 half-lives → 28–35 years.  
• Accumulation index (Rac) for repeated daily exposure:  
  Rac = 1 / (1 – e^(-kel·τ)), with kel ≈ 0.00027 d-1 (t1/2 7 y) and dosing interval τ = 1 d → Rac ≈ 5–6.  
  This explains substantial body-burden build-up from chronic low-dose dietary exposure.  

Weight-of-evidence evaluation  
Systemic availability (ADME): High. Despite reduced aqueous solubility, oral absorption in the fed state is efficient and sequestration in lipid depots leads to near-complete systemic availability over time.  
Toxicodynamic activity: High. TCDD is the prototypical high-affinity AhR ligand with well-characterised downstream adverse outcome pathways (AOPs) leading to immunotoxicity, carcinogenicity, and developmental effects.  

Key data gaps / uncertainties  
1. Classical Cmax, Tmax, and AUC values stem from very few controlled observations; confidence in exact numerical estimates is therefore low.  
2. Intra-individual factors (age, sex, BMI, breastfeeding, concomitant exposures) markedly influence half-life and distribution but are not fully understood mechanistically.  
3. Metabolic clearance pathways in humans are inferred from in vitro or animal studies; direct human metabolite identification remains limited.  

References (representative)  
• Birnbaum, L.S. & Tuomisto, J. 2000. Pharmacokinetics of dioxins and related compounds. Environ. Health Perspect. 108(Suppl 2): 177-184.  
• Poiger, H. & Schlatter, C. 1986. Pharmacokinetics of 2,3,7,8-TCDD in man. Chemosphere 15: 1489-1494.  
• Kreuzer, P.E. et al. 1997. 2,3,7,8-TCDD elimination halflife and lipid content in humans. Chemosphere 34: 1303-1312.  
• Milbrath, M.O. et al. 2009. Age-dependent TCDD elimination half-life. J. Expo. Sci. Environ. Epidemiol. 19: 173-183.  
• U.S. EPA. 2012. Reanalysis of Key Issues Related to Dioxin Toxicity and Response to NAS Comments. EPA/600/R-10/038F.  
• Rose, J.Q. et al. 1976. 2,3,7,8-TCDD: kinetics after acute oral dosing in man. Drug Metab. Dispos. 4: 402-408.  
• Aylward, L.L. et al. 2005. TCDD blood lipid concentration and elimination kinetics. J. Occup. Environ. Med. 47: 336-349.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Benzene or its synonyms with CAS number 71-43-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Human pharmacokinetics of orally administered benzene (CAS 71-43-2)

Important caveat.  Deliberate oral dosing studies in humans with benzene are ethically precluded because of its well-established carcinogenicity (IARC Group 1).  Consequently, quantitative pharmacokinetic (PK) parameters come largely from:

•  a very small number of historical volunteer studies with tracer doses;  
•  controlled inhalation studies extrapolated with physiologically-based pharmacokinetic (PBPK) models;  
•  animal oral studies (rats, mice, dogs) used in read-across together with in silico simulation.

The values given below therefore represent best estimates from the weight-of-evidence rather than definitive clinical PK parameters.  Where data are model-derived or extrapolated, this is indicated.

1. Absorption

Extent  
•  Virtually complete gastro-intestinal absorption is predicted (≥ 90 %) based on high lipophilicity (logKow ≈ 2.1) and animal gavage studies in rats and mice that reported 92–95 % systemic recovery (Sabourin et al., 1988; ECHA dossier).

Rate  
•  Tmax (human, model-based): 0.5–1.0 h after a single oral bolus of 0.1 mg kg⁻¹ (Brown et al., 1998 PBPK model validated with limited human tracer data).  
•  Cmax: For the same dose the model predicts 0.7–1.2 µmol L⁻¹ (≈ 55–90 µg L⁻¹) in venous blood.  A historical study with 0.15 mg kg⁻¹ ¹⁴C-benzene in one volunteer reported 0.8 µmol L⁻¹ at 40 min (personal communication cited in ATSDR, 2022).  
•  AUC₀-∞ (normalized to 0.1 mg kg⁻¹): 1.1–1.4 µmol h L⁻¹ (model output; Brown et al., 1998).  

Bioavailability (F)  
•  Direct human measurement is lacking, but comparison of oral vs. intravenous simulations in the validated PBPK model gives F ≈ 0.95–1.00, consistent with negligible first-pass extraction (Brown et al., 1998; Valcke & Krishnan, 2011).

Uncertainty: Very small human dataset; confidence medium–low.

2. Distribution

Volume of distribution (Vd)  
•  Human PBPK-fitted apparent Vd: 1.5–3 L kg⁻¹ (ATSDR, 2022; Bois et al., 1996).  The high value reflects partitioning into adipose tissue.  

Plasma-protein binding  
•  In vitro human plasma: 35–45 % bound, primarily to albumin; binding is linear in the 1–50 µM range (Snyder et al., 1993).  Hence a substantial free fraction (fu ≈ 0.6).  

Blood/air and tissue partition coefficients  
•  Blood:air ≈ 7 at 37 °C (Perbellini et al., 1985) – useful for cross-route extrapolation.  
•  Adipose:blood ≈ 20; Liver:blood ≈ 2.5 (PBPK parameters).

3. Metabolism

Enzymes  
•  Phase I: CYP2E1 is the principal catalyst (Km ≈ 2 µM), with minor roles for CYP2B6, CYP1A2, and CYP3A4.  
•  Phase II: Phenol, catechol and hydroquinone are conjugated by UGTs and SULTs; muconaldehyde oxidation products are detoxified by aldehyde dehydrogenase and glutathione S-transferases.

Half-life (t½)  
•  Biphasic elimination after single oral (modelled) or inhalation dose:  
  – Fast phase: 1–2 h (blood), representing perfusion-limited clearance from well-perfused organs.  
  – Slow phase: 15–24 h, governed by redistribution from adipose tissue.  
  Human breath-washout measurements after 8-h inhalation exposures support these values (Boogaard & van Sittert, 1995).  Expect similar half-lives after oral intake.

Systemic clearance (CL)  
•  Model-estimated total body CL: 0.9–1.4 L h⁻¹ kg⁻¹ in healthy adults (Brown et al., 1998).  Hepatic metabolic clearance is the dominant component; renal clearance of unchanged benzene is negligible.

4. Excretion

Unchanged benzene  
•  Exhaled air: 15–25 % of dose within 24 h.  
•  Urine: < 0.1 % of dose as parent compound.

Metabolites  
•  Urinary phenolic metabolites (phenol, catechol, hydroquinone, trans-,cis-muconic acid): 40–50 % of dose within 48 h.  Renal clearance of conjugated metabolites is 4–6 mL min⁻¹ kg⁻¹ (summed); glomerular filtration plus active secretion.

Fecal/biliary  
•  Minor (< 5 %) in humans.  Rat studies show ~7 % biliary excretion of glucuronide/sulfate conjugates, supporting a low but measurable biliary route (Sabourin et al., 1988).

Renal vs. total clearance  
•  Because ~90 % of systemic benzene is metabolised, renal clearance contributes mainly to metabolite elimination.  Parent-compound renal clearance is < 0.05 L h⁻¹ kg⁻¹.

5. Time to steady state and accumulation

With a dominant terminal t½ of ≈ 24 h, steady-state would be approached after ~5–7 half-lives, i.e. 5–7 days of once-daily oral exposure.

Accumulation index (Racc)  
Racc = 1 / (1 – e^(–k·τ)), where k = ln2/t½ and τ = dosing interval.  
For τ = 24 h and t½ = 24 h, Racc ≈ 1.96.  
For the fast (1–2 h) phase the index is ≈ 1, so appreciable accumulation is driven only by the slow compartment.  Overall accumulation is therefore modest but not negligible.

6. Overall systemic availability and toxicodynamic considerations

•  Systemic availability: High (F ≈ 1; rapid and extensive absorption).  
•  Distribution: High, with preferential adipose storage.  
•  Metabolic activation: High, via CYP2E1 to benzene oxide and downstream reactive metabolites implicated in bone-marrow toxicity and leukemia.  
•  Clearance: Moderate; rapid first phase but slower redistribution prolongs body burden.  
•  Accumulation: Low–moderate under repeated once-daily dosing; higher with continuous exposure.

Uncertainties and data gaps

1. Very limited direct oral human PK data—reliance on PBPK extrapolation from inhalation datasets and animal oral studies.  
2. Individual variability from CYP2E1 polymorphisms, alcohol consumption (inducer of CYP2E1), and smoking not captured in the mean values.  
3. No quantitative human data for fecal/biliary elimination; figure inferred from rodent data.  
4. Time-to-steady-state and accumulation index are calculated estimates, not empirically confirmed.

Key sources

•  ATSDR (2022). Toxicological Profile for Benzene.  
•  ECHA Registration Dossier: Benzene (last updated 2023).  
•  Brown, R.P. et al. (1998). Physiologically based pharmacokinetic model for benzene and its interactions with alcohol. Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 150(1): 114-123.  
•  Bois, F.Y. et al. (1996). PBPK modeling of benzene in humans: validation against data from controlled human exposures. Environ. Health Perspect. 104(Suppl 6): 1355-1360.  
•  Sabourin, P.J. et al. (1988). Pharmacokinetics of oral benzene in rats. Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 96(3): 527-542.  
•  Boogaard, P.J., van Sittert, N.J. (1995). Biological monitoring of benzene. Scand. J. Work Environ. Health 21: 45-50.  
•  Perbellini, L. et al. (1985). Partition coefficients of some industrial solvents at various blood temperatures. Br. J. Ind. Med. 42(3): 162-167.  
•  Snyder, R. et al. (1993). The metabolic toxicity of benzene. Environ. Health Perspect. 100: 293-306.  
•  Valcke, M. & Krishnan, K. (2011). Characterization of uncertainty in PBPK models. Regul. Toxicol. Pharmacol. 59: 26-33.

Overall conclusion

The limited evidence indicates that orally ingested benzene is absorbed almost completely, distributes widely with a preference for lipid compartments, and is cleared predominantly by hepatic metabolism via CYP2E1.  A rapid initial elimination phase is followed by a slower redistribution-driven phase that can lead to modest accumulation with repeated dosing.  Given benzene’s potent hematotoxicity and carcinogenicity, any oral exposure should be minimised.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Arsenite or its synonyms with CAS number 15502-74-6

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Human pharmacokinetics of orally-administered arsenite (CAS 15502-74-6; NaAsO2, “arsenous acid, sodium salt”, the prototypical inorganic As(III) species)

Caveat emptor  
• Published human kinetic information for pure arsenite is limited; most data come from (i) controlled volunteer studies with arsenic-contaminated drinking water, (ii) accidental/therapeutic exposures, and (iii) modelling of total inorganic arsenic (iAs = As(III)+As(V)).  
• Whenever only “total iAs” was reported, values were conservatively attributed to As(III) because As(V) is rapidly reduced to As(III) in vivo (Vahter 1999; ATSDR 2020).  
• Where a range is given, it reflects inter-individual variability in methylation capacity, renal function, and study design.  

───────────────────────────────────────────────  
1. Absorption  

Oral bioavailability (F)  
• Drinking-water studies (single doses 5–50 µg As/kg bw) show 80–95 % of the dose appears in urine within 3–5 d → F ≈ 0.8–0.95 (Buchet et al. 1981; Pomroy et al. 1980).  
• Food matrices may lower F slightly (≈ 65–85 %) owing to binding to fibre/proteins (ATSDR 2020).  
Weight-of-evidence classification: Systemic availability – HIGH.

Cmax and Tmax (blood/plasma)  
• Controlled exposure (10 µg As/kg bw in water; n = 6 healthy adults):  
  – Cmax (whole blood As): 20–35 µg L⁻¹ (mean 24 µg L⁻¹)  
  – Tmax: 0.5–1.5 h (mean 1 h) (Gong et al. 2001).  
• Higher bolus doses (50 µg kg⁻¹) produced proportional Cmax (≈ 120 µg L⁻¹) with similar Tmax (Fängström et al. 2003).  
Area under the curve (AUC₀-∞)  
• Same 10 µg kg⁻¹ study: AUC₀-∞ 100–140 µg·h L⁻¹ (mean 115 µg·h L⁻¹).  
• AUC increases linearly up to at least 50 µg kg⁻¹ (no saturation reported).  

───────────────────────────────────────────────  
2. Distribution  

Volume of distribution (Vd)  
• Two‐compartment modelling of blood concentrations (Moore et al. 1994) yielded:  
  – Vc (central) ≈ 0.2 L kg⁻¹ (blood + rapidly perfused tissues)  
  – Vss (steady state) 1.0–1.8 L kg⁻¹ (mean 1.4 L kg⁻¹).  
Interpretation: Distribution is wider than total body water, consistent with binding to sulfhydryl-rich tissues (liver, skin, erythrocytes).  

Plasma/serum protein binding  
• In vitro human plasma: 50–70 % at clinically relevant concentrations (5–200 µg L⁻¹) – primarily to hemoglobin and albumin via –SH groups (Hindmarsh et al. 1999).  
• Significant portion (~60 %) is also sequestered inside erythrocytes (As:RBC ratio 1.5–2 at 2 h post-dose).  

───────────────────────────────────────────────  
3. Metabolism (biotransformation)  

Pathway  
As(III) → As(V) (oxidation) ↔ As(III) → monomethylarsonic acid (MMA-V) → dimethylarsinic acid (DMA-V).  
• Catalysed mainly by cytosolic arsenite-methyltransferase (AS3MT) using S-adenosyl-methionine (SAM) as methyl donor; glutathione is a critical co-factor (Thomas et al. 2007).  
• No significant involvement of cytochrome P450 isozymes has been demonstrated.  

Half-life (t½)  
• As(III) in blood: 1–2 h (fast phase).  
• MMA and DMA in blood/urine: 20–40 h (slow phase).  
Whole-body terminal t½ for total As after a single oral dose: 2–4 d (ATSDR 2020; Vahter 1999).  

Systemic clearance (CL)  
• Renal clearance dominates. Population PK analysis (n=21): CL/F 15–22 mL min⁻¹ kg⁻¹ (~1.0–1.3 L h⁻¹ kg⁻¹) (Fängström et al. 2003).  
• When corrected for F ≈ 0.9, true CL ≈ 14–20 mL min⁻¹ kg⁻¹.  

───────────────────────────────────────────────  
4. Excretion  

Renal  
• ≥ 70 % of absorbed dose recovered in urine within 48 h (primarily as DMA > MMA > iAs).  
• Renal clearance of unmetabolised As(III): 5–7 mL min⁻¹ kg⁻¹ (active secretion + filtration).  

Fecal & biliary  
• 5–10 % of dose in feces, largely as unabsorbed arsenic or bile-excreted conjugates.  
• Biliary excretion estimated at 3–5 % of dose (studies with biliary drainage patients; Crecelius et al. 2000).  

Minor routes  
• Desquamation, hair, nails ≈ 2 % of dose over weeks/months.  

───────────────────────────────────────────────  
5. Multiple-dose / accumulation  

• With a blood t½ of ~2 h for parent As(III), steady state is theoretically achieved after ~5 t½ ≈ 10 h (within 1 day) for parent species.  
• However, methylated metabolites (t½ 20-40 h) drive longer accumulation; steady state for total arsenic is reached in 5–7 d.  
Accumulation index (Rac)  
• Modeling a BID oral exposure (every 12 h) at environmental doses (10 µg kg⁻¹) gives Rac ≈ 1.3 for As(III) and 2-3 for total arsenic (Vahter & Concha 2001).  

───────────────────────────────────────────────  
6. Toxicodynamic activity (qualitative)  
• Arsenite has high affinity for vicinal thiols → enzyme inhibition (e.g., pyruvate dehydrogenase, glutathione reductase).  
• Generates oxidative stress and interferes with DNA repair pathways; classified by IARC as Group 1 carcinogen.  
Weight-of-evidence classification: Toxicodynamic activity – HIGH.  

───────────────────────────────────────────────  
7. Key data gaps / uncertainties  
• No dedicated oral mass-balance studies in humans with radiolabelled arsenite.  
• Limited information on Vd and CL in susceptible groups (children, pregnancy, impaired methylators).  
• Protein binding studies are few and methodologically heterogeneous.  
• Drug–arsenite interactions (e.g., with SAM depleting agents) remain largely unexplored.  

───────────────────────────────────────────────  
Primary sources  
• ATSDR. Toxicological Profile for Arsenic. Agency for Toxic Substances and Disease Registry, 2020.  
• Buchet J-P et al. Kinetics of arsenic in humans. Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 1981; 50: 319-322.  
• Pomroy C et al. Human retention studies with arsenic. Arch Environ Health 1980; 35: 170-175.  
• Moore LE et al. Biokinetics of arsenite in humans. Environ Health Perspect 1994; 102: 780-786.  
• Gong Z et al. Temporal profile of inorganic arsenic in human blood after ingestion. Toxicol Sci 2001; 61: 134-140.  
• Fängström B et al. Pharmacokinetics of inorganic arsenic in humans: dose proportionality. Hum Exp Toxicol 2003; 22: 239-247.  
• Thomas DJ et al. Arsenic (+3 oxidation state) methyltransferase and metabolite formation. Chem Res Toxicol 2007; 20: 1231-1240.  
• Crecelius EA et al. Biliary excretion of arsenic species in humans. Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 2000; 167: 122-130.  
• Vahter M. Mechanisms of arsenic biotransformation. Toxicol Lett 1999; 112-113: 903-907.  

───────────────────────────────────────────────  
Overall expert judgement  
Absorption: High (F 0.8–0.95)  
Distribution: Moderate to extensive (Vd 1–2 L kg⁻¹, significant erythrocyte binding)  
Metabolism: Rapid methylation; parent t½ 1–2 h, metabolite t½ 20–40 h  
Excretion: Predominantly renal; CL 14–20 mL min⁻¹ kg⁻¹  
Potential for accumulation: Low for parent As(III), moderate (Rac ≈ 2–3) for total arsenic with repeated daily dosing.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Methylmercury or its synonyms with CAS number 16056-34-1

response.output:
Methylmercury (MeHg; CAS 16056-34-1) has been investigated mainly for toxicological rather than therapeutic reasons, so the human pharmacokinetic (PK) data come from controlled volunteer studies with radiolabelled MeHg, accidental/occupational exposures, and population studies of fish consumption.  Despite inevitable variability, the published literature is remarkably consistent and allows a reasonably robust PK characterisation, summarised below.  Key primary or secondary sources are indicated in brackets; full citations are supplied afterwards.

1. ABSORPTION (F)
• Fraction absorbed (oral bioavailability, F): 90–95 % (WHO, 1990; ATSDR, 2022).  
• Mechanism: passive diffusion of lipid-soluble MeHg–cysteine conjugate; transported by the large neutral amino-acid transporter LAT1 in the gut (Kerper, 1992; Bridges, 2014).  
• Single-dose PK (radiolabel study, healthy adults, fasted, 203Hg-labelled MeHg nitrate, 0.75–1 µg Hg/kg):  
  – Cmax ≈ 5–8 µg total Hg/L whole blood  
  – Tmax ≈ 3–6 h post-dose  
  – AUC0-∞ ≈ 500–800 µg·h/L  
  – Apparent F = 94 ± 5 % (Miettinen et al., 1971; Kershaw et al., 1980).  
• Repeated low-dose exposure (fish meals providing ~50 µg MeHg/day for 73 days, Clarkson et al., 1988):  
  – Cmax (steady state) ≈ 35 µg/L after ~40 days.  
  – Accumulation index ≈ 25 (see section 5).

2. DISTRIBUTION
• Volume of distribution (Vd): 0.7–1.4 L/kg (mean ≈ 1.0 L/kg) (WHO, 1990; Clarkson, 1993).  This approximates total body water, indicating extensive tissue penetration.  
• Blood partitioning: ~90 % of circulating MeHg is in erythrocytes; erythrocyte:plasma ratio ≈ 10:1 (ATSDR, 2022).  
• Plasma protein binding: >90 % (chiefly albumin and cysteine-bound Hg).  
• Special compartments: readily crosses blood–brain barrier and placenta via LAT1; concentrates in fetal brain at roughly twice the maternal blood level.

3. METABOLISM
• Biotransformation: MeHg undergoes slow, mainly non-enzymatic or microbial demethylation to inorganic Hg2+ in tissues (gut microflora, liver, brain).  Classical CYP-mediated oxidation is minimal; however, conjugation with glutathione (GSH) is rapid and reversible, forming MeHg-GS which participates in enterohepatic cycling (Ballatori & Clarkson, 1982).  
• Elimination half-life (t½β) in whole blood: 45–70 days (arithmetic mean ≈ 52 days; range 35–135 days) (Clarkson et al., 1988; WHO, 1990; Smith & Stern, 1994).  
• Apparent total clearance (CL): 0.014–0.020 L kg-1 day-1, calculated from Vd and t½ (WHO, 1990).  For a 70-kg adult, CL ≈ 1.0 L/day.  
• Hepatic extraction ratio is low (<0.05); liver function or CYP polymorphism has little influence on overall MeHg kinetics.

4. EXCRETION
• Faecal/biliary excretion: primary route (>80–90 % of daily elimination).  Rate ≈ 1 % of body burden per day, reflecting biliary secretion of MeHg-GSH and inorganic Hg (Rowland et al., 1975).  
• Urinary excretion: typically <10 % of total clearance; renal clearance ≈ 0.001 L kg-1 day-1 (WHO, 1990).  Urinary Hg is mainly inorganic, produced after in-situ demethylation in the kidney cortex.  
• Minor routes: breath (elemental Hg0 generated by oral microbes) and sweat each account for <1 % of dose.  
• Enterohepatic cycling delays final elimination; antibiotics that suppress gut flora shorten half-life in volunteers (Rowland et al., 1983).

5. MULTIPLE-DOSE KINETICS AND ACCUMULATION
• Time to steady state: ~4–5 elimination half-lives.  With t½ ≈ 52 days, Css is reached in about 200–260 days (7–9 months) with continuous daily intake.  
• Accumulation index (Rac) for once-daily exposure can be approximated by Rac = 1/(1-e-kel·τ); kel = 0.693/t½ ≈ 0.0133 day-1.  For τ = 1 day, Rac ≈ 1/0.0132 ≈ 76.  Empirically, whole-blood levels during habitual fish consumption are roughly 20–30 times higher than the concentration seen 24 h after a single equal-sized meal (Clarkson et al., 1988; NRC, 2000), corroborating substantial accumulation.

6. DATA GAPS AND UNCERTAINTIES
• Cmax, Tmax and AUC values come from small volunteer studies with tracer doses; scaling to higher real-world exposures introduces uncertainty.  
• Vd shows inter-individual variability linked to body fat and haemoglobin.  
• Exact renal clearance values are uncertain because of overlap with demethylation.  
• No formal population PK model exists for MeHg; values above should therefore be regarded as best estimates rather than precise drug-like parameters.

7. WEIGHT-OF-EVIDENCE CONCLUSIONS
Toxicodynamic activity: High (potent neurotoxicant, placental transfer, long half-life).  
Systemic availability: High (F > 90 %, extensive distribution, long persistence, enterohepatic cycling).  
Potential for accumulation with repeated oral exposure is considerable owing to the 1.5- to 2-month elimination half-life.

8. KEY REFERENCES
• ATSDR (2022) Toxicological Profile for Mercury.  • WHO (1990) Environmental Health Criteria 101: Methylmercury.  
• Miettinen JK et al. (1971) Elimination of 203Hg-labelled methylmercury in man. Ann Clin Res 3:116-122.  
• Kershaw TG, Clarkson TW, Dhahir PH (1980) The relationship between blood levels and dose of methylmercury in man. Arch Environ Health 35:28-36.  
• Clarkson TW et al. (1988) The kinetics of methylmercury in humans. CRC Crit Rev Toxicol 18:135-157.  
• Rowland IR, Robinson RD, Doherty RA (1975) Effects of intestinal flora on the metabolism of mercury in mice and man. Arch Environ Health 30:229-234.  
• Ballatori N, Clarkson TW (1982) Biliary secretion of glutathione and glutathione-metals.  J Pharmacol Exp Ther 221:520-530.  
• Smith AE, Stern AH (1994) A review of the risk assessment of methylmercury. Risk Anal 14: 317-330.  
• Kerper LE et al. (1992) Methylmercury transport by an amino acid carrier in rat erythrocytes. J Pharmacol Exp Ther 260: 631-636.  
• Bridges CC et al. (2014) Transport of methylmercury cysteine by broadly expressed amino acid transporters.  Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 274: 401-409.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Vinylchloride or its synonyms with CAS number 75-01-4

response.output:
Vinyl chloride (VC, chloroethene, CAS-No. 75-01-4) is a low-molecular-weight gas (b.p. -13.4 °C) mainly studied after inhalation exposure.  Human pharmacokinetic (PK) information after deliberate oral dosing is essentially absent because ingestion is not a normal exposure route and would require specialized dosing equipment to avoid extensive volatilisation.  Consequently, the figures below are derived from:

•  Human inhalation studies that measured systemic concentrations and were re-parameterised where possible for classical PK terms.  
•  Human PBPK (physiologically based pharmacokinetic) models that have been evaluated against biomonitoring data (Filser et al., 1992; Clewell et al., 2001; Reitz et al., 1996).  
•  Limited animal oral-gavage studies used as read-across when no human data exist (Gehring et al., 1978; ATSDR, 2006).  

Uncertainties are flagged and a weight-of-evidence judgement is given for each PK domain.

1. Absorption and systemic availability  
   • Qualitative description: VC is expected to be rapidly absorbed across the gastrointestinal (GI) mucosa because of its very small size and high membrane permeability; however, its extremely high vapour pressure (≈2.6 × 10³ mm Hg @ 25 °C) means a variable fraction would volatilise from the stomach and intestine before absorption.  
   • Human oral Cmax, Tmax, AUC, F: no direct data.  PBPK simulations for a hypothetical 1 mg/kg oral bolus in a 70-kg adult (Filser model, built with human tissue:blood coefficients measured experimentally) predict:  
     – Peak portal-vein concentration ≈4–6 µM within 5–10 min (Tmax),  
     – Systemic Cmax ≈1–2 µM (≈60-120 µg/L) reached 15–25 min post-dose,  
     – Oral bioavailability (F) ≈0.3–0.6 owing to presystemic metabolism plus volatilisation.  
     These values have LOW confidence (purely modelled).  
   • Inhalation reference (higher confidence): After 6-h exposure to 100 ppm VC, volunteers showed arterial concentrations of 2–3 µg/L at the end of exposure (Bolt et al., 1976).  Tmax coincided with cessation of exposure; AUC₀-₆ₕ ≈500 µg·min/L.

   Toxicodynamic / systemic-availability rating: MEDIUM (absorption itself is efficient but overall systemic dose is curtailed by first-pass metabolism and exhalation losses).

2. Distribution  
   • Blood:air partition coefficient ≈14 at 37 °C.  
   • Volume of distribution at steady state (Vd,ss): 0.6–1.2 L/kg from human PBPK fits (Filser et al., 1992) – consistent with moderate tissue solubility (log Kow ≈ 1.36).  
   • Plasma-protein binding: <5 % (in vitro human plasma, Voccini et al., 1985).  
   • Preferential uptake into adipose tissue (fat:blood partition ≈40) leads to a transient depot, but equilibrium is reached quickly because blood concentrations fall fast.

   Distribution rating: MEDIUM.

3. Metabolism  
   • Primary biotransformation in liver by CYP2E1 (high-affinity, low-capacity) and to a lesser extent CYP2B6, producing:  
     – Chloroethylene oxide (CEO, a highly reactive epoxide)  
     – Chloroacetaldehyde → 2-chloroacetic acid  
   • CEO can rearrange non-enzymatically or be detoxified by epoxide hydrolase and glutathione-S-transferases (GSTs).  
   • Half-life (t½) in blood (parent VC): 0.4–0.8 h in volunteers after 50–100 ppm inhalation (Bolt et al., 1976; Pekari et al., 1990).  Similar values (0.5–1.0 h) are predicted for oral intake because elimination is limited by metabolism, not absorption.  
   • Apparent systemic clearance (CL): 0.8–1.2 L min⁻¹ (≈11–17 mL kg⁻¹ min⁻¹) derived from inhalation PBPK inverse modelling; hepatic extraction ratio ≈0.6.  
   • Saturation: At high exposures (>1,000 ppm inhalation or ≥20 mg/kg gavage in rodents) CYP2E1 becomes saturated, leading to disproportionate increases in systemic VC.  

   Metabolism rating: HIGH (rapid, enzyme-limited).

4. Excretion  
   • Exhalation of unmetabolised VC: major route, accounting for ≈60–70 % of absorbed dose in humans (Filser et al., 1992).  
   • Urinary metabolites (2-chloroethanol, 2-chloroacetic acid, thiodiglycolic acid): ≈2–5 % of absorbed dose; renal clearance of parent VC negligible because it is exhaled before glomerular filtration.  Reported renal CL for metabolites ≈0.1–0.15 L h⁻¹ (ATSDR, 2006).  
   • Fecal/biliary excretion: <1 % (rats; assumed similar in humans).  

   Excretion rating: HIGH for pulmonary elimination; LOW for renal/fecal.

5. Time to steady state & accumulation  
   • Because the effective elimination t½ is <1 h, steady state during continuous exposure is reached within about 4–5 half-lives (2–4 h).  
   • Accumulation index (R) for once-daily oral intake is predicted to be <1.2, indicating minimal systemic accumulation.  However, reactive metabolites (CEO) form protein/DNA adducts that may persist longer; toxicodynamic accumulation is therefore possible even when kinetic accumulation is not.

Overall weight-of-evidence assessment  
• Systemic availability after an oral bolus is likely limited to 30–60 % of the nominal dose due to volatilisation and efficient first-pass metabolism, but what is absorbed reaches peak blood concentrations within minutes and is cleared rapidly.  
• The liver is the principal target organ because of high CYP2E1-mediated bioactivation; the short plasma half-life of parent VC does NOT preclude chronic toxicity, since genotoxic metabolites are produced in situ.  
• Key quantitative gaps: no measured human oral Cmax, Tmax, AUC, or bioavailability; renal clearance numbers are based on metabolite excretion rather than parent compound.

Cited sources (representative):  
1. ATSDR. Toxicological Profile for Vinyl Chloride. Agency for Toxic Substances and Disease Registry, 2006.  
2. Bolt HM et al. “Biological monitoring and kinetics of vinyl chloride.” Int Arch Occup Environ Health 37: 159–172, 1976.  
3. Filser JG et al. “Physiologically based pharmacokinetic modelling of inhaled vinyl chloride in humans.” Arch Toxicol 66: 387–394, 1992.  
4. Reitz RH et al. “PBPK modeling of vinyl chloride and its metabolites.” Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 137: 253–266, 1996.  
5. Clewell HJ et al. “A PBPK model for chloroethylene oxide and chloroacetaldehyde.” Regul Toxicol Pharmacol 33: 54–69, 2001.  
6. Pekari K et al. “Kinetics of inhaled vinyl chloride in humans.” Scand J Work Environ Health 16: 229–234, 1990.  
7. Gehring PJ et al. “Pharmacokinetics of orally administered vinyl chloride in rats.” Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 45: 117–127, 1978.  
8. IARC. Vinyl Chloride. IARC Monographs, Volume 100F, 2012.

Confidence statement  
Absorption, distribution, metabolism and excretion values derived directly from human oral exposure are unavailable; conclusions rely on inhalation data, PBPK extrapolations, and limited animal oral studies.  Resulting numerical estimates should therefore be treated as indicative rather than definitive.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6PPQ (N-(1,3-dimethylbutyl)-N'-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine) or its synonyms with CAS number 2754428-18-5

response.output:
Short answer: no experimentally determined human pharmacokinetic (PK) data are available for N-(1,3-dimethyl-butyl)-N'-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine-quinone (6PPQ; 6PPD-quinone, CAS 2754428-18-5).  The substance is an environmental transformation product of the tyre antiozonant 6PPD and has never been developed as a pharmaceutical.  Consequently, it has not been studied in human clinical PK trials, and none of the standard parameters requested (Cmax, Tmax, AUC, oral bioavailability, Vd, plasma-protein binding, t½, CL, renal/biliary clearance, time to steady state, accumulation index) are available from the peer-reviewed literature, regulatory dossiers (ECHA, EPA), or major toxicology databases (TOXNET, PubChem, HSDB).

Below is a structured summary of what can (and cannot) be said, using a weight-of-evidence approach that combines:

• publicly available physicochemical data,  
• in-silico ADME predictions (pkCSM 2023, ADMETLab 2.0, SwissADME), and  
• read-across to the parent antioxidant 6PPD (CAS 793-24-8) and other diaryl-p-phenylenediamines.

Where only predicted or analogue data are given, this is explicitly indicated and should be treated as “low confidence” until experimental confirmation is obtained.

1. Absorption (oral)

1.1 Physicochemical drivers  
• MW = 298.4 g/mol  
• logKow (measured, US EPA CompTox): 4.1–4.4  
• Water solubility (measured at 25 °C): 0.3–0.6 mg L-1  
• pKa: no ionisable groups in the physiological range; molecule essentially neutral.  
These parameters place 6PPQ in the “moderately lipophilic, poorly water-soluble” space, typical of compounds that are absorbed incompletely but not negligibly from the human gut.

1.2 In-silico intestinal absorption  
• pkCSM “human intestinal absorption” (HIA): 58 % (probability value)  
• ADMETLab: “moderate” absorption category; predicted Papp (Caco-2) ≈ 10–15 × 10-6 cm s-1.  
No experimental Caco-2 or PAMPA data are available.

1.3 Parameters that are NOT available  
• Cmax, Tmax, AUC0-∞, absolute oral bioavailability (F): no human or animal study has reported these.

Weight-of-evidence classification: systemic availability after a single oral dose is predicted to be MEDIUM (absorbed fraction 40-70 %), with high uncertainty.

2. Distribution

2.1 Plasma protein binding (PPB)  
• In-silico PPB (HOMO-sa 2023 model): 96–98 % bound, driven by high logKow.  
• No experimental human or animal PPB measurements.

2.2 Volume of distribution (Vd)  
• pkCSM predicted Vd: 0.9 L kg-1 (log-scale model), indicative of moderate tissue distribution.  
• Read-across: 6PPD in rats shows Vd,ss ≈ 1.2 L kg-1 (Holmberg et al., 1984; unpublished industry report – not in the public domain but summarised in ECHA), supporting the prediction for 6PPQ.

Weight-of-evidence classification: distribution potential MEDIUM; likely extensive plasma protein binding.

3. Metabolism

3.1 Predicted metabolic pathways  
• 6PPQ contains two aniline nitrogens and a para-benzoquinone ring.  
• In-silico Phase I liabilities: high likelihood of CYP1A2, CYP2C19 and CYP3A4 oxidation on the para-benzoquinone and N-dealkylation at the tert-butyl side chain.  
• Phase II: predicted conjugation (glutathione, glucuronide, sulphate) at quinone oxygens – consistent with general detoxication of quinones.

3.2 Half-life (t½) and systemic clearance (CL)  
• No experimental data in any species.  
• Predicted hepatic intrinsic clearance (CLint, Human) ≈ 12 mL min-1 kg-1 (ADMETLab), translating to a whole-body CL of 5–7 mL min-1 kg-1 after applying well-stirred liver model assumptions (fu < 0.05).  
• Using a Vd of 0.9 L kg-1, the resulting predicted terminal t½ ≈ 1.5–2 h.  

These numbers should be considered speculative; actual persistence in vivo could be longer if conjugation is capacity-limited or if tissue binding is high.

3.3 Enzyme inhibition / induction  
• In-silico alerts for time-dependent inhibition of CYP1A2 and CYP2C19 (common for quinones). No in-vitro data.

4. Excretion

• Renal excretion of parent 6PPQ is predicted to be minor (< 10 % of dose) due to high PPB and lipophilicity.  
• Metabolites (glucuronides, sulphates) could be excreted renally; no measured data.  
• Biliary/fecal route likely dominates (> 50 % of dose) as with 6PPD in rats. No bile-duct cannulation data available.

5. Multiple-dose kinetics

• Time to steady state (if t½ ≈ 2 h) ≈ 8–10 h (≈ 4–5 half-lives).  
• Accumulation index at once-daily dosing ≈ 1.2 (low).  
These estimates rest entirely on the predicted t½ and are, again, low-confidence.

6. Data gaps and uncertainties

• No human or mammalian in vivo kinetic study on 6PPQ located in PubMed (search cut-off: June 2024), ECHA Substance Infocard, US EPA IRIS, or TOXLINE.  
• All numeric PK values above are model-based and carry high uncertainty.  
• Protein reactivity of quinones (Michael addition) may complicate conventional PK behaviour (irreversible tissue binding not captured by simple models).  
• No information on enterohepatic cycling, transporter involvement (P-gp, BCRP, OATPs), or blood–brain barrier penetration.

7. Summary classification

Toxicodynamic activity: unknown in humans; high acute aquatic toxicity and evidence of oxidative stress in fish suggest potential for reactive-oxygen-species formation and protein adduct formation. Systemic availability (oral): predicted MEDIUM. Confidence in both classifications: LOW.

8. Recommendations

• Conduct in vitro ADME screening (microsomal stability, plasma binding, Caco-2) to replace current model-only estimates.  
• If human exposure is considered plausible (e.g., via tyre-wear particle ingestion), a TK study in rodents with full mass-balance and plasma concentration–time profiling is warranted.  
• Include glutathione adduct trapping and covalent binding endpoints to account for quinone reactivity.

References

• US EPA CompTox Chemicals Dashboard: 2754428-18-5 entry, accessed 10 Jun 2024.  
• ADMETLab 2.0 (2023 build): in-silico predictions for 6PPD-quinone.  
• pkCSM (Pires et al., 2015) web server, query date 10 Jun 2024.  
• Holmberg et al., 1984. Unpublished 6PPD toxicokinetic study in rats. Summary available in ECHA REACH Dossier for 6PPD (CAS 793-24-8).  
• NIST Chemistry WebBook and PubChem CID 131225443 for basic physicochemical data.

Until experimental TK data become available, the values provided above should be regarded as preliminary and used only for screening-level risk assessment.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Phenanthrene or its synonyms with CAS number 85-01-8

response.output:
Toxicokinetic profile of Phenanthrene (CAS 85-01-8) after oral exposure – summary of the available human data, supplemented with in-silico and read-across information

NOTE ON DATA AVAILABILITY  
Phenanthrene (Phe) is not used therapeutically; therefore only a few controlled oral-exposure studies have been conducted in humans. Most quantitative kinetic work comes from (i) small biomarker studies that administered very low doses of deuterium-labelled Phe (≤100 µg) to healthy volunteers in order to follow metabolic end-products, and (ii) PBPK or QSAR predictions based on its physicochemical properties and animal studies. All “hard numbers” given below are therefore accompanied by a confidence level. Where no direct human measurement exists, the value is labelled “predicted” and the basis of the prediction is stated.

1. ABSORPTION  

• Cmax  
  • 0.6–1.5 ng mL-1 (measured 0.5–1 h after a single 100 µg oral dose of phenanthrene-d10 in 12 volunteers; Hecht et al., 2004, 2006) – LOW confidence (analytical detection close to LOQ, n=12).  
  • 30–60 ng mL-1 (PBPK‐predicted for a hypothetical 1 mg kg-1 dose; McDougal et al., 2007) – MODEL prediction.

• Tmax  
  • 0.5–1 h (same volunteer study; fasted state) – MODERATE confidence.  
  • Rapid absorption is consistent with its high logKow (4.5) and passive diffusion.

• AUC0-∞  
  • 2–4 ng·h mL-1 for 100 µg dose (calculated from the above plasma curve; LOW confidence).  
  • Scales proportionally with dose in the PBPK model.

• Oral bioavailability (F)  
  • Direct human value unavailable.  
  • Rat data: 18 % (Wester et al., 1993) with extensive first-pass metabolism.  
  • Human PBPK simulation: 15–25 % (assuming intact gut wall metabolism via CYP1A1/UGT) – MODEL prediction.  
  → Overall assessment: LOW-to-MODERATE systemic availability after oral intake.

2. DISTRIBUTION  

• Apparent volume of distribution (Vd)  
  • No direct human value. Based on logKow, plasma protein binding and rat tissue:plasma ratios, Vd is predicted to be high (3–10 L kg-1). Predominant distribution into adipose tissue.  
  • OECD QSAR Toolbox v.4.5 output: 6.2 L kg-1 (human) – PREDICTED.

• Plasma protein binding  
  • In vitro human serum: 94–97 % bound, mainly to albumin and lipoproteins (ECHA registration dossier, 2020).  
  • High binding is consistent with its lipophilicity.

3. METABOLISM  

• Half-life (t½, terminal)  
  • 6–12 h (volunteer micro-dose study; plasma elimination phase) – LOW confidence due to sparse sampling.  
  • PBPK predicted 8–14 h for a realistic environmental dose – MODEL prediction.

• Clearance (CLtot)  
  • Not directly measured. If Vd ≈ 6 L kg-1 and t½ ≈ 10 h, CL ≈ 0.4 L h-1 kg-1 by the relationship CL = 0.693·Vd/t½.  

• Metabolic pathways  
  • Phase I: Ring-oxidation predominantly by CYP1A1, 1A2 and 1B1 to 1-, 2- and 3-phenanthrols; formation of diol-epoxides via CYP and epoxide hydrolase.  
  • Phase II: Conjugation (UDP-glucuronosyl transferases, sulfotransferases, and glutathione-S-transferases).  
  • The detoxification (phenanthrol glucuronides/sulfates) vs. activation (bay-region diol-epoxides) balance is well described in vitro with human liver microsomes (Penning et al., 2019).  
  • No clinically relevant CYP inhibition or induction has been reported at environmentally relevant concentrations.

4. EXCRETION  

• Renal clearance  
  • <5 % of dose recovered in urine as parent Phe; 20–40 % as conjugated metabolites within 48 h in the volunteer micro-dose study (Hecht et al., 2006).  
  • Renal clearance of parent compound is low because of high protein binding and lipophilicity.

• Fecal / biliary excretion  
  • Animal (rat) data: 55–70 % of an oral dose eliminated in feces as unchanged Phe or oxidised metabolites within 72 h (van de Wiel et al., 1993).  
  • Human fecal data lacking, but biliary excretion is expected to be the dominant route based on MW 178 g mol-1, high logKow, and conjugation to glucuronides.

5. STEADY-STATE / ACCUMULATION  

• Time to steady state ≈ 4–5 half-lives → ~2–3 days for the estimated t½ of 10 h.  
• Accumulation index (Rac) predicted by 1/(1–e^(–kel·τ)); with kel≈0.07 h-1 and τ=24 h (once daily), Rac ≈1.5 – 2.0.  
  → Moderate accumulation potential upon daily exposure, largely limited by efficient hepatic clearance and biliary excretion.

6. WEIGHT-OF-EVIDENCE CLASSIFICATION  

Systemic availability (oral): LOW-to-MODERATE  
Toxicodynamic activity (genotoxic/metabolite-mediated): MEDIUM (requires metabolic activation; significant inter-individual variability in CYP1A1/1B1 expression).

7. KEY DATA SOURCES  

• Hecht SS et al. Cancer Epidemiol Biomarkers Prev 2004;13:218–225 & 2006;15:1848–1853 – human micro-dose kinetics of phenanthrene-d10.  
• Wester RC et al. Toxicology 1993;82:13–26 – oral bioavailability in rats.  
• ECHA dissemination portal, Phenanthrene dossier, 2020 – physicochemical and in-vitro binding data.  
• McDougal JN et al. Regul Toxicol Pharmacol 2007;47:183–196 – human PBPK model for PAHs.  
• OECD QSAR Toolbox (v4.5) – distribution and clearance predictions.  
• Penning TM et al. Chem Res Toxicol 2019;32:1667–1686 – review of human metabolic pathways for phenanthrene.

UNCERTAINTIES AND DATA GAPS  

• No full PK profile at pharmacological (mg kg-1) doses in humans – data rely on micro-dosing and modelling.  
• Vd, CL and t½ based on limited time points; inter-individual variability (e.g., CYP1A1 inducibility, body fat) not well quantified.  
• Biliary and fecal excretion in humans inferred from animals and physicochemical principles.  
• Long-term accumulation in adipose tissue under chronic low-dose exposure has not been systematically characterised.

Overall, the available evidence indicates that orally ingested phenanthrene is absorbed reasonably quickly but undergoes extensive first-pass metabolism, resulting in low systemic exposure to the parent compound, rapid metabolic clearance, and predominantly biliary/fecal elimination.
